# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hooke's Law from Linear Elasticity

Starting from the **fundamental definition** of stress and strain in uniaxial tension:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{P}{A_0} \\
\varepsilon &= \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation (Robert Hooke, 1660):**
Within the elastic range, the elongation ∆L is proportional to the applied load P:

$$
\Delta L \;\propto\; P
$$

**Step 2 — Convert proportionality to equality** by introducing a constant of proportionality k (which depends on the geometry and material):

$$
\Delta L = \frac{P L_0}{A_0 E}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange** to introduce stress and strain:

$$
\frac{P}{A_0} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}
$$

**Step 4 — Recognise the terms** as stress and strain:

$$
\boxed{\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Meaning:**
E is the slope of the **initial linear portion** of the σ–ε curve. For shipbuilding steel, **E = 205 GPa (≈ 2.05 × 10⁵ MPa)** regardless of grade. This is because E is governed by interatomic bond stiffness, which is similar for all steels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship between E, G, and ν

Consider a small cubic element subjected to a pure shear stress τ. By the **complementary shear stresses theorem**, shear on a face produces equal and opposite shear on perpendicular faces.

For a linear elastic isotropic material, the shear strain γ is related to τ by:

$$
\tau = G \cdot \gamma
$$

Using **tensor transformation of strains** (textbook derivation from elasticity theory):

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{1}{G}\,\tau_{xy}
$$

The **generalised Hooke's Law** for isotropic materials gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &= \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] \\
\varepsilon_y &= \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right] \\
\gamma_{xy} &= \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **pure shear state** (σ_x = σ_y = +τ, σ_z = 0, with principal stresses at 45°):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_x &= \frac{1}{E}\left[\tau - \nu(\tau + 0)\right] = \frac{\tau(1-\nu)}{E} \\
\gamma_{xy} &= 2\varepsilon_x = \frac{2\tau(1-\nu)}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with τ = G·γ_xy:

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}
$$

**Verification for steel** (E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.30):

$$
G = \frac{210}{2(1+0.30)} = \frac{210}{2.6} \approx 80.77 \text{ GPa}
$$

This matches experimental values for shipbuilding steel. ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: True Stress from Engineering Stress (Volume Constancy)

During plastic deformation, the material's volume is assumed to remain constant (incompressibility):

$$
A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i
$$

**Step 1 — Express current area in terms of current length:**

$$
A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_i}
$$

**Step 2 — Engineering strain** in terms of lengths:

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{L_i - L_0}{L_0} \quad\Rightarrow\quad L_i = L_0(1 + \varepsilon)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** into the area expression:

$$
A_i = \frac{A_0 L_0}{L_0(1 + \varepsilon)} = \frac{A_0}{1 + \varepsilon}
$$

**Step 4 — True stress** is force divided by instantaneous area:

$$
\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_i} = \frac{P}{A_0/(1+\varepsilon)} = \frac{P}{A_0}\,(1+\varepsilon)
$$

**Step 5 — Recognise** that P/A₀ is the engineering stress σ:

$$
\boxed{\sigma_t = \sigma\,(1 + \varepsilon)}
$$

**Step 6 — True strain** (natural logarithm form):

$$
\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_i} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_i}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
> A mild steel specimen with original diameter **d₀ = 12 mm** and gauge length **L₀ = 50 mm** is tested. At a certain load, the engineering stress is **350 MPa** and engineering strain is **0.18**. Find true stress and true strain.
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \sigma &= 350 \text{ MPa} \\
> \sigma_t &= 350 \times (1 + 0.18) = 350 \times 1.18 = \mathbf{413 \text{ MPa}} \\
> \varepsilon_t &= \ln(1 + 0.18) = \ln(1.18) = \mathbf{0.1655} \;\;(\text{dimensionless})
> \end{aligned}
> $$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Modulus of Resilience

The modulus of resilience U_r is the **strain energy per unit volume** stored elastically — i.e., the area under the elastic portion of the σ–ε curve (a triangle for linear elastic material).

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_r &= \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} \sigma \, d\varepsilon \\
&= \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} E\varepsilon \, d\varepsilon \\
&= E \left[\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\right]_0^{\varepsilon_y} = \frac{E \varepsilon_y^2}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Using Hooke's Law at yield: σ_y = E·ε_y, so ε_y = σ_y / E:

$$
U_r = \frac{E}{2}\!\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ship-design relevance:** Resilience is the ability of steel to absorb **impact energy** elastically (e.g., slamming, berthing). For a typical ship steel with σ_y = 235 MPa and E = 210 GPa:
>
> $$
> U_r = \frac{(235 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 210 \times 10^9} \approx 0.131 \text{ MJ/m}^3
> $$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Mild Steel vs High-Tensile Steel

| Parameter | Mild Steel (Grade A) | High-Tensile Steel (AH36) |
|---|---|---|
| σ_y (yield) | 235 MPa | 355 MPa |
| σ_u (ultimate) | 400–490 MPa | 490–620 MPa |
| σ_u / σ_y (reserve ratio) | ~1.8 | ~1.5 |
| Elongation (%) | 22–28% | 18–22% |
| Yield-point phenomenon | **Yes** (sharp yield) | **No** (rounded, 0.2% proof) |
| Charpy impact @ 0 °C | 27 J | 34 J |
| Cost (relative) | 1.0× | 1.15–1.25× |
| Weight saving in hull | baseline | 15–20% |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good (slight preheat may be needed) |
| Typical ship use | Bottom, side shell, bulkheads | Deck, sheer strake, hatch corners |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Differentiate between mild steel and high-tensile steel used in shipbuilding. Give any two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Mild Steel | High-Tensile Steel |
|---|---|---|
| Yield strength | 235 MPa (min) | 315–355 MPa (min) |
| Examples | Grade A, Grade B, Grade D, Grade E | AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36 |
| Yield behaviour | Sharp yield point (upper/lower) | No distinct yield; 0.2% proof stress used |
| Elongation | 22–28% | 18–22% |
| Weldability | Excellent | Good (slight preheat for thicker plates) |
| Application | Hull bottom, side shell, bulkheads | Deck plating, sheer strake, hatch coaming |
| Cost | Lower | 15–25% higher |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Set B]**
**Define the following terms: (i) Yield strength, (ii) Ultimate tensile strength, (iii) Percentage elongation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks = 1 mark each):**

(i) **Yield Strength (σ_y):** The stress at which a material begins to deform plastically. For mild steel, it is taken at the **lower yield point**; for high-tensile steel (without a yield point), it is the **0.2% proof stress**.

(ii) **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_u):** The maximum engineering stress a material can withstand before the onset of necking. It is computed as the **peak load divided by the original cross-sectional area**.

(iii) **Percentage Elongation:** A measure of ductility, defined as the permanent increase in gauge length after fracture, expressed as a percentage of the original gauge length:

$$
\%\,\text{Elongation} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100
$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module 1):** Two 14-mark questions, each with two 7-mark sub-parts. Internal choice: Student answers **either** Q(A) **or** Q(B).

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**A(a) [7 marks]** Draw a fully labelled stress–strain diagram for **mild steel** under uniaxial tension. Explain the significance of each region.

**A(b) [7 marks]** A mild steel specimen of original diameter **15 mm** and gauge length **60 mm** is tested in tension. The specimen yields at a load of **66 kN** and fractures at a load of **110 kN**. Final diameter at fracture is **9 mm** and final gauge length is **78 mm**. Calculate: (i) Yield strength, (ii) Ultimate tensile strength, (iii) % Elongation, (iv) % Reduction in area.

**Model Solution:**

**A(a) — Diagram and explanation:** *(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the diagram. The student should draw and label 5 regions: Elastic, Yield, Plastic (Strain hardening), Ultimate, Necking/Fracture. 1 mark for diagram, 1 mark per region explanation = 5 marks, plus 1 mark for naming key stress values σ_y, σ_u, σ_f.)*

**A(b) — Numerical solution:**

**Step 1 — Original cross-sectional area:**

$$
A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4} d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(15)^2 = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Yield Strength (i):**

$$
\sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{66 \times 10^3}{176.71} = 373.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (ii):**

$$
\sigma_u = \frac{P_u}{A_0} = \frac{110 \times 10^3}{176.71} = 622.4 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage Elongation (iii):**

$$
\%\,\text{El} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{78 - 60}{60} \times 100 = \mathbf{30\%}
$$

**Step 5 — Final area at fracture:**

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4} d_f^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(9)^2 = 63.62 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Percentage Reduction in Area (iv):**

$$
\%\,\text{RA} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{176.71 - 63.62}{176.71} \times 100 = \mathbf{64\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kN to N** (multiply by 10³). This single error can wipe out 3–4 marks.
> 2. Using **final area** for ultimate stress instead of **original area** — ultimate stress is always computed with **A₀**, not A_f.
> 3. Confusing **engineering stress** with **true stress** in part (b). KTU almost always asks for engineering stress unless explicitly stated.
> 4. Forgetting units in the final answer. Always state "MPa" or "GPa" explicitly.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Module 1, Alt Choice]**

**B(a) [7 marks]** Compare the stress–strain behaviour of **mild steel** and **high-tensile (HSLA) steel**. Why is the **0.2% proof stress** used in design for HSLA steel?

**B(b) [7 marks]** For a ship hull plate of mild steel, the following data are given:
- Modulus of elasticity: E = **210 GPa**
- Yield strength: σ_y = **240 MPa**
- Density: ρ = **7850 kg/m³**

Calculate: (i) The modulus of resilience. (ii) The strain at yield. (iii) The strain energy stored per cubic metre of plate at the proportional limit. (iv) If Poisson's ratio ν = 0.30, determine the shear modulus G.

**Model Solution:**

**B(a) — Comparison:**

| Aspect | Mild Steel | HSLA Steel |
|---|---|---|
| Yield point | Distinct (upper/lower) | Not distinct; smooth transition |
| Design yield value | Lower yield point | 0.2% proof stress (σ_0.2) |
| Yield strength | 235 MPa | 315–355 MPa |
| Ductility | Higher | Slightly lower |
| Use | General hull | High-stress regions |

**Reason for 0.2% proof stress:**
HSLA steel lacks a sharp yield point. Hence, by convention (ISO 6892-1, ASTM A370), the **0.2% offset method** is used: a line parallel to the elastic slope is offset by 0.2% strain along the strain axis. The intersection with the σ–ε curve defines σ_0.2, which represents the stress that produces a small but defined amount of permanent strain — analogous to the yield point in mild steel. *(4 marks explanation, 3 marks comparison table)*

**B(b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Modulus of resilience:**

$$
U_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(240 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 210 \times 10^9} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^{16}}{4.2 \times 10^{11}} = \mathbf{0.1371 \; MJ/m^3}
$$

**(ii) Strain at yield:**

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{240 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9} = \mathbf{1.143 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

**(iii) Strain energy per m³ at proportional limit:**
Since the proportional limit is very close to the yield point in mild steel, the strain energy density ≈ U_r = **0.1371 MJ/m³**.

**(iv) Shear modulus:**

$$
G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210}{2(1+0.30)} = \frac{210}{2.6} = \mathbf{80.77 \; GPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. In part (b)(i), the **0.2 in the formula is the denominator, not a strain** — students often mis-apply. Recheck: U_r = σ_y² / (2E), **not** σ_y² × 0.2.
> 2. Always write E in **Pa (not MPa)** when substituting into the resilience formula unless you balance units: σ in Pa gives U_r in J/m³.
> 3. For G, write it as **GPa** (or kN/mm²) to match the input units of E.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 (PCNST502):**

- ☐ **Steel** = Fe + C (0.05–2%) + alloying elements. Used for **>90% of world fleet** by tonnage.
- ☐ **Mild steel** (Grade A/B/D/E): σ_y ≥ **235 MPa**, σ_u = **400–490 MPa**, exhibits **sharp yield point**.
- ☐ **High-tensile steel** (AH36, DH36, EH36): σ_y ≥ **355 MPa**, no yield point → use **0.2% proof stress**.
- ☐ **Suffix meanings:** A = As-rolled, D = Normalised, E = TMCP. The number (32, 36, 40) corresponds to the **tensile-strength grade in ksi × 10**.
- ☐ **Stress–strain curve regions (5):** Elastic → Upper Yield → Lower Yield → Strain Hardening → Necking → Fracture.
- ☐ **Key stress values:** σ_y (yield), σ_u (ultimate), σ_f (fracture). Design is based on **σ_y**, not σ_u.
- ☐ **Hooke's Law:** σ = Eε, valid only in the **proportional/elastic region**.
- ☐ **Young's Modulus of steel** = **205–210 GPa**, almost **constant** for all steel grades.
- ☐ **Poisson's ratio** of steel = **0.30** → G = **E / [2(1+ν)] ≈ 80 GPa**.
- ☐ **True stress** = σ(1+ε); **True strain** = ln(1+ε). Used in **post-necking / FEA plasticity** analysis.
- ☐ **0.2% proof stress** = stress at intersection of σ–ε curve and a line offset by 0.002 strain parallel to the elastic slope.
- ☐ **Resilience** = σ_y² / (2E). **Toughness** = area under the full σ–ε curve.
- ☐ **Ductility measures:** % Elongation, % Reduction in Area. Higher values = better collision energy absorption.
- ☐ **Classification society standards** (IACS, ABS, LR, DNV, IRS) prescribe minimum **Charpy V-notch** impact values for low-temperature service.
- ☐ **Units to use in KTU exam:** Stress in **MPa (N/mm²)**, Modulus in **GPa**, Energy in **J or MJ**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory aid (for the night before the exam):**
> *"Steel is the ship's skeleton — its σ–ε curve is the X-ray that tells you how strong, stiff, and tough the skeleton really is."*

---

topics.title: Higher Tensile Steel - Properties and Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Higher Tensile Steel - Properties and Application # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Higher Tensile Steel (HTS)**, in the context of ship structural engineering, refers to a category of carbon-manganese and micro-alloyed structural steels possessing a specified minimum **yield strength** substantially greater than that of ordinary mild shipbuilding steel, typically ranging from **315 N/mm²** to **390 N/mm²**, while still retaining adequate ductility, weldability, notch-toughness, and formability for marine service conditions. These steels are governed by the rules of international Classification Societies such as **Lloyd's Register (LR), American Bureau of Shipping (ABS), Bureau Veritas (BV), Det Norske Veritas (DNV), and Indian Register of Shipping (IRS)**, and conform to standards such as **IACS Unified Requirements UR W (UR S6/W1 to W27)** for hull structural steels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > Higher tensile steel is treated as the *advanced alternative* to mild steel for modern ship construction. Students are expected to know its grades, mechanical properties, classification society designations, and specific applications in the hull. ## 1.2 Designation System (IACS Standard) The most widely used HTS grades in shipbuilding are designated by an **alphanumeric code** as follows: | Letter Code | Meaning | Delivery Condition | |-------------|---------|-------------------| | **A** | Grade with normal strength | As-rolled / Normalized | | **D** | Grade with improved toughness | Quenched & Tempered (Q&T) | | **E** | Grade with highest toughness | Thermo-Mechanically Controlled Processed (TMCP) | The **numerical suffix** indicates the minimum yield strength in **ksi × 10** units (or, equivalently, the kg/mm² level in older notation): - **32 → 32 kgf/mm² ≈ 315 N/mm² (MPa)** - **36 → 36 kgf/mm² ≈ 355 N/mm² (MPa)** Hence, the grade **AH36** signifies: **A**-quality steel with minimum yield strength of **355 N/mm²**. > [!NOTE] > The exact yield values in SI units are: > - Grade 32 series: $\sigma_y \geq 315$ **N/mm²** (MPa) > - Grade 36 series: $\sigma_y \geq 355$ **N/mm²** (MPa) > - Mild steel (Grade A) reference: $\sigma_y \geq 235$ **N/mm²** (MPa) ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine two identical aluminium ladders being used to climb onto a ship hull. One ladder is made of a thin, cheap aluminium (like *mild steel*) — it bends easily and feels flimsy when a heavy worker steps on it. The other ladder is made of a slightly heavier but markedly stiffer special alloy (like *higher tensile steel*) — it does not bend, supports a much higher load, and is also significantly lighter than a thicker mild-steel ladder that would carry the same load. **HTS behaves exactly like this analogy for ships:** by using a steel of higher yield strength, the shipbuilder can reduce the *thickness* of plates and stiffeners, thereby **reducing the displacement (light-ship weight)**, **increasing the cargo deadweight**, and **improving the ship's stability and speed**, all while maintaining (or even increasing) the structural safety factor. ## 1.4 Why Higher Tensile Steel is Used in Modern Ships - **Weight reduction** in the hull (typically **15 % to 25 %** lighter than equivalent mild-steel structure). - **Increased payload / deadweight** without enlarging the principal dimensions. - **Improved structural performance** under combined loading (sagging, hogging, slamming). - **Reduced plate thickness** simplifies fabrication, reduces welding volume, and lowers overall cost despite higher per-tonne steel cost. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanical Property Requirements (Classification Society Rules) For HTS to be acceptable in primary hull structure, it must satisfy the following (typical for AH/DH/EH 32, 36 grades): | Property | Typical Specified Value | Test Standard | |----------|------------------------|---------------| | Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$) | $\geq 315$ MPa (32-grade) or $\geq 355$ MPa (36-grade) | EN 10025 / ASTM A131 | | Ultimate Tensile Stress ($\sigma_u$) | $440 - 590$ MPa (32-grade) or $490 - 620$ MPa (36-grade) | EN 10025 | | Elongation ($\% E$) | $\geq 22\%$ on $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{S_0}$ gauge | EN 10002 | | Charpy V-Notch Impact | $\geq 34$ J at $0°$C (A-grade), $-20°$C (D-grade), $-40°$C (E-grade) | EN 10045 | | Cold Bend (180°) | No cracks on $r = 2t$ bend radius | EN ISO 7438 | ## 2.2 Chemical Composition (Typical, % by mass) | Element | Mild Steel (Grade A) | HTS Grade 32 | HTS Grade 36 | |---------|--------------------|-------------|-------------| | Carbon (C) | $\leq 0.21$ | $\leq 0.18$ | $\leq 0.18$ | | Manganese (Mn) | $0.50 - 1.20$ | $0.90 - 1.60$ | $0.90 - 1.60$ | | Silicon (Si) | $0.10 - 0.35$ | $0.10 - 0.50$ | $0.10 - 0.50$ | | Sulphur (S) | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | | Phosphorus (P) | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | $\leq 0.035$ | | Micro-alloys (Nb, V, Ti) | — | Added in trace amounts | Added in trace amounts | > [!NOTE] > The **low carbon equivalent (CEV $\leq 0.45$ for 36-grade)** ensures good weldability, while micro-alloying elements refine the grain and provide precipitation strengthening. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|--------------------|-----------------|-------| | 1 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_y}{A}$ | Yield stress definition | N/mm² | | 2 | $t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}}$ | Equivalent thickness conversion | mm | | 3 | $\Delta W = \rho \cdot A \cdot (t_{MS} - t_{HTS})$ | Weight saving per plate | kg | | 4 | $CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Carbon Equivalent Value (IIW) | dimensionless | | 5 | $T_{preheat} = f(CEV, t_{plate})$ | Minimum preheat for welding | °C | | 6 | $f_{s} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\rho \cdot g}$ | Specific strength index | m | | 7 | $P_{mg} = \rho \cdot g \cdot t$ | Mass per unit area of plate | kg/m² | where: - $F_y$ = yield force (N) - $A$ = cross-sectional area (mm²) - $t_{HTS}, t_{MS}$ = thickness of HTS and mild steel plate (mm) - $\sigma_{y,MS} = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_{y,HTS} = 315$ or $355$ MPa - $\rho$ = density of steel $\approx 7850$ **kg/m³** - $g$ = $9.81$ **m/s²** ## 2.4 Operational Logic of HTS Use — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Define the design load** (bending moment, shear, pressure) acting on the plate or stiffener. 2. **Calculate the required section modulus** $Z = M / \sigma_{allow}$. 3. Since $\sigma_{allow}$ for HTS is higher (315 or 355 MPa vs 235 MPa for mild steel), the **required $Z$ decreases**. 4. For the same plate width, **$t$ may be reduced** according to the square-root relationship. 5. Reduced $t$ leads to **lower displacement** → higher payload capacity. 6. Verify **stability** (against buckling), **fatigue**, and **corrosion allowance** — these often *govern* over plain yield. 7. Confirm **weldability** via CEV and preheat temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > The reduction in thickness is **not linear** with yield strength. Because bending stress varies linearly with distance from the neutral axis, the required section modulus scales as $1/\sigma_y$, and thickness as $1/\sqrt{\sigma_y}$. This is a *classic KTU-board favourite question*. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Equivalent Thickness of an HTS Plate **Problem statement:** A mild-steel plate of thickness $t_{MS}$ carries a bending moment $M$ per unit width. Find the equivalent thickness $t_{HTS}$ in higher tensile steel (yield $\sigma_{y,HTS}$) that produces the *same maximum bending stress* under the same moment. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Bending stress formula (per unit width, for a plate strip of width $b=1$):** $$ \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} $$ where $Z$ is the section modulus per unit width. **Step 2 — Section modulus of a rectangular plate strip of thickness $t$:** $$ Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6} $$ For unit width, $b=1$, hence $$ Z = \dfrac{t^{2}}{6} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum bending stress in mild-steel plate:** $$ \sigma_{max,MS} = \dfrac{M \cdot 6}{t_{MS}^{2}} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{MS}^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum bending stress in HTS plate (same moment $M$):** $$ \sigma_{max,HTS} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{HTS}^{2}} $$ **Step 5 — Equate the two stresses (design requirement: same elastic strength utilisation):** $$ \dfrac{6M}{t_{MS}^{2}} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{HTS}^{2}} $$ But because the allowable stress for HTS is *higher* by the ratio $\sigma_{y,HTS}/\sigma_{y,MS}$, we instead require: $$ \dfrac{6M}{t_{HTS}^{2}} = \dfrac{6M}{t_{MS}^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}} $$ **Step 6 — Simplify and solve for $t_{HTS}$:** $$ \dfrac{1}{t_{HTS}^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{t_{MS}^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}} $$ Taking the square root of both sides: $$ t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}} $$ This is the **master formula** for HTS thickness selection, and is heavily tested in KTU examinations. ### Numerical Illustration Given $t_{MS} = 20$ mm, $\sigma_{y,MS} = 235$ MPa, $\sigma_{y,HTS} = 355$ MPa (AH36): $$ t_{HTS} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{235}{355}} $$ Compute the ratio: $$ \dfrac{235}{355} = 0.6620 $$ Take the square root: $$ \sqrt{0.6620} = 0.8136 $$ Hence: $$ t_{HTS} = 20 \times 0.8136 = 16.27 \text{ mm} $$ In practice, this is rounded **up** to the next standard plate thickness, typically $t_{HTS} = 16$ mm or $17$ mm depending on the yard's standard. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Weight Saving per Plate The mass per square metre of a steel plate is: $$ P_{mg} = \rho \cdot t $$ where $\rho = 7850$ **kg/m³** and $t$ is in metres. For our numerical example: $$ \Delta t = t_{MS} - t_{HTS} = 20 - 16.27 = 3.73 \text{ mm} = 0.00373 \text{ m} $$ Weight saved per square metre: $$ \Delta P_{mg} = \rho \cdot \Delta t = 7850 \times 0.00373 = 29.28 \text{ kg/m}^{2} $$ Percentage saving: $$ \%\text{ Saving} = \dfrac{\Delta t}{t_{MS}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.73}{20} \times 100 = 18.65\% $$ > [!NOTE] > A typical HTS application yields **15 % – 25 %** weight saving in the affected structure — perfectly consistent with our calculated $18.65\%$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) — Welding Suitability The IIW (International Institute of Welding) formula is: $$ CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ For a typical AH36 with composition $C=0.16\%$, $Mn=1.45\%$, $Si=0.30\%$, $S=0.02\%$, $P=0.02\%$: $$ CEV = 0.16 + \dfrac{1.45}{6} + 0 + 0 $$ $$ CEV = 0.16 + 0.2417 = 0.4017 $$ This is **well below** the 0.45 limit, confirming excellent weldability. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Where HTS is Used vs Not Used in a Ship | Hull Region | Material Commonly Used | Reason | |-------------|----------------------|--------| | Bottom plating (turn of bilge) | AH36 / DH36 | High bending + slamming | | Side shell (sheer strake) | AH36 / DH36 | High shear + cyclic wave load | | Deck plating (weather deck) | AH36 / DH36 | Deck bending, deck impact | | Bulkhead plating | A-grade or AH32 | Lower stress, cost saving | | Superstructure | A-grade (mild steel) | Light stress, ease of fabrication | | Rudder, shaft brackets | EH36 / DH36 | High concentrated stress | | Stem, stern frame | Cast steel / EH36 | Impact and torsion | | Tank tops (oil tanker) | AH32 / AH36 | Sloshing + corrosion | | Hatch coamings | AH36 | Localised high stress | | Mast, king posts | Mild steel | Non-critical secondary structure | ## 3.5 Verification of Design (KTU Exam-Style Logic) A complete KTU-style verification requires three checks: 1. **Yield check:** $\sigma_{max} \leq \sigma_y / SF$ (where $SF$ is the partial safety factor, typically $1.0$ for steel in IACS scantlings). 2. **Buckling check:** For plates in compression, the critical stress $$\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k \pi^{2} E}{12 \left(1 - \nu^{2}\right)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$$ must exceed the applied stress. 3. **Fatigue check:** For $N \geq 10^{7}$ cycles, the S-N curve for HTS gives allowable $\Delta\sigma \approx 73$ MPa at the hot-spot. > [!IMPORTANT] > In *no case* should the plate thickness be reduced below the **corrosion addition** (typically 0.5 to 1.0 mm per side) plus the **mill tolerance** (typically 0.3 to 0.8 mm). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — HTS Classification & Application Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Ship Structural Steel]:::root --> B[Normal Strength
Mild Steel
Grade A]:::node1 A --> C[Higher Tensile Steel
HTS]:::node2 A --> D[High Strength
EH47 and above]:::node3 B --> B1[Yield 235 MPa]:::sub C --> C1[Grade 32 Series
Yield 315 MPa]:::sub C --> C2[Grade 36 Series
Yield 355 MPa]:::sub C1 --> C1A[AH32
A-quality
Normalized]:::sub C1 --> C1B[DH32
D-quality
Q and T]:::sub C1 --> C1C[EH32
E-quality
TMCP]:::sub C2 --> C2A[AH36
A-quality
Normalized]:::sub C2 --> C2B[DH36
D-quality
Q and T]:::sub C2 --> C2C[EH36
E-quality
TMCP]:::sub C2A --> APP1[Deck Plating]:::app C2A --> APP2[Bottom Plating]:::app C2A --> APP3[Sheer Strake]:::app C2B --> APP4[Hatch Coaming]:::app C2B --> APP5[Rudder Horn]:::app C2C --> APP6[Critical Bow Region]:::app C2C --> APP7[Stern Frame]:::app classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef node1 fill:#4a7ba6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef node2 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef node3 fill:#7d3c98,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef sub fill:#f5e6c8,stroke:#5d4e37,color:#000 classDef app fill:#27ae60,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — HTS Plate Selection Logic (KTU Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Identify the structural
member and design load]:::s --> S2[Step 2: Calculate required
section modulus Z required]:::s S2 --> S3[Step 3: Select material grade
Mild Steel or HTS]:::s S3 --> Q1{Is yield strength
critical criterion?}:::q Q1 -- Yes --> S4[Use HTS Grade 32 or 36
Higher sigma y]:::s Q1 -- No --> S5[Use Mild Steel Grade A]:::s S4 --> S6[Step 4: Compute equivalent
thickness using square root formula]:::s S6 --> S7[Step 5: Add corrosion
allowance and mill tolerance]:::s S7 --> Q2{Is buckling
critical?}:::q Q2 -- Yes --> S8[Step 6: Increase thickness
or add stiffeners]:::s Q2 -- No --> S9[Step 7: Check fatigue
and weldability]:::s S9 --> S10[Step 8: Finalize thickness
to next standard size]:::s classDef s fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef q fill:#e67e22,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of HTS Property Relationships ```mermaid flowchart LR CHEM[Chemical Composition
C, Mn, Si, Nb, V, Ti]:::root --> PROC[Processing Route
Normalized / Q and T / TMCP]:::root PROC --> GRAIN[Refined Grain
Structure]:::root GRAIN --> MECH[Mechanical
Properties]:::root CHEM --> MECH MECH --> YS[Yield Strength 315 or 355 MPa]:::prop MECH --> UTS[UTS 440 to 620 MPa]:::prop MECH --> ELONG[Elongation 22 percent]:::prop MECH --> IMPACT[Charpy Impact 34 J]:::prop YS --> APPL[Shipboard
Application]:::app UTS --> APPL ELONG --> APPL IMPACT --> APPL APPL --> A1[Deck Plating]:::item APPL --> A2[Bottom Plating]:::item APPL --> A3[Side Shell]:::item APPL --> A4[Hatch Coaming]:::item classDef root fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef prop fill:#16a085,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef app fill:#d35400,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef item fill:#f39c12,stroke:#fff,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The diagrams above are intentionally designed as **block-level functional architecture** representations rather than physical drawings, in line with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback protocol for non-geometric topics. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Define Higher Tensile Steel. List any four grades of HTS used in shipbuilding with their minimum yield strength values.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Higher Tensile Steel (HTS) is a structural steel used in ship construction having a minimum specified yield strength considerably higher than ordinary mild steel (Grade A), in the range of **315 N/mm² to 390 N/mm²**, with adequate ductility, weldability, and notch-toughness for marine service. *(1 mark)* Four common grades of HTS with minimum yield strength: *(2 marks)* | Grade | Delivery Condition | Min. Yield Strength (N/mm²) | |-------|-------------------|-----------------------------| | AH32 | Normalized | 315 | | DH32 | Quenched & Tempered | 315 | | AH36 | Normalized | 355 | | EH36 | Thermo-mechanically processed | 355 | --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *What is the significance of the Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) in higher tensile steel? State the IIW formula for CEV.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Significance:** The Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) is a numerical index that quantifies the combined effect of alloying elements on the **weldability** of steel. A lower CEV indicates better weldability and lower risk of **hydrogen-induced cracking (HIC)** in the heat-affected zone (HAZ). For HTS, the CEV is typically limited to **0.45** (for grade 36) to ensure satisfactory weldability using standard shipyard procedures. *(2 marks)* **IIW Formula:** $$ CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$ where the symbols denote weight percentage of the respective elements. *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 1]`** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **alphanumeric designation system** for HTS plates as per IACS Unified Requirements. List the minimum yield strength, ultimate tensile strength, and impact-test temperature for grades **AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, and EH36**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A ship side-shell plate of **20 mm** thick mild steel (Grade A, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa) is to be replaced with HTS Grade AH36 ($\sigma_y = 355$ MPa). Calculate: - (i) The equivalent thickness of AH36 plate. - (ii) The weight saving per square metre of plating. - (iii) The percentage reduction in thickness. Take $\rho_{steel} = 7850$ kg/m³. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution to Question A **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The IACS Unified Requirement **UR W11** (formerly S6) defines the alphanumeric designation as follows: 1. **First letter** — *Quality grade based on notch toughness and impact-test temperature:* - **A** → tested at $0°$C, normalised condition - **D** → tested at $-20°$C, quenched & tempered - **E** → tested at $-40°$C, thermo-mechanically controlled processed (TMCP) 2. **Suffix numerals** — *Strength grade based on minimum yield stress:* - **32** → minimum yield 315 N/mm² - **36** → minimum yield 355 N/mm² - **40** → minimum yield 390 N/mm² (newer grade) **Tabulated properties:** *(4 marks for table)* | Grade | Min. Yield (N/mm²) | UTS (N/mm²) | Impact Test Temp (°C) | |-------|--------------------|-------------|----------------------| | AH32 | 315 | 440 – 590 | 0 | | DH32 | 315 | 440 – 590 | –20 | | EH32 | 315 | 440 – 590 | –40 | | AH36 | 355 | 490 – 620 | 0 | | DH36 | 355 | 490 – 620 | –20 | | EH36 | 355 | 490 – 620 | –40 | *(3 marks for design explanation, list & impact logic)* --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $t_{MS} = 20$ mm - $\sigma_{y,MS} = 235$ N/mm² - $\sigma_{y,HTS} = 355$ N/mm² - $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³ **(i) Equivalent thickness of AH36 plate:** *(3 marks)* Apply the thickness-conversion formula derived in Section 3.1: $$ t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}} $$ Substitute values: $$ t_{HTS} = 20 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{235}{355}} $$ Compute the ratio: $$ \dfrac{235}{355} = 0.6620 $$ Take square root: $$ \sqrt{0.6620} = 0.8136 $$ Therefore: $$ t_{HTS} = 20 \times 0.8136 = 16.27 \text{ mm} $$ In practice, this is rounded up to the next standard plate size of **17 mm** (or as per yard standard). **(ii) Weight saving per square metre:** *(2 marks)* Mass per m² of mild steel plate: $$ m_{MS} = \rho \cdot t_{MS} = 7850 \times 0.020 = 157.0 \text{ kg/m}^{2} $$ Mass per m² of HTS plate (using 16.27 mm): $$ m_{HTS} = 7850 \times 0.01627 = 127.72 \text{ kg/m}^{2} $$ Weight saving: $$ \Delta m = 157.0 - 127.72 = 29.28 \text{ kg/m}^{2} $$ **(iii) Percentage reduction in thickness:** *(2 marks)* $$ \%\text{ reduction} = \dfrac{t_{MS} - t_{HTS}}{t_{MS}} \times 100 $$ $$ = \dfrac{20 - 16.27}{20} \times 100 = \dfrac{3.73}{20} \times 100 = 18.65\% $$ **Conclusion:** Replacing mild steel with AH36 results in approximately **18.65 %** reduction in plate thickness and a weight saving of **29.28 kg per square metre**, leading to a lighter hull and higher deadweight capacity. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Module 1]`** **(a)** Compare **mild steel (Grade A)** and **higher tensile steel (Grade AH36)** under the following heads: - (i) Minimum yield strength - (ii) Carbon content and Carbon Equivalent Value - (iii) Weldability - (iv) Cost per tonne - (v) Typical shipboard applications - (vi) Specific strength (strength-to-weight ratio) *(7 marks)* **(b)** A bulk carrier has a **bottom-plating** region designed with mild steel of **22 mm** thickness. As a weight-saving measure, the shipowner wants to use **DH36** ($\sigma_y = 355$ N/mm²) for the same region. Calculate the **percentage reduction in lightship weight** contributed by this plating, and the **deadweight gain** if the affected plating area is **1500 m²**. Take mild steel yield strength = 235 N/mm², density = 7850 kg/m³. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution to Question B **Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks, 1 mark per head + 1 mark for specific strength calculation):** | Parameter | Mild Steel (Grade A) | Higher Tensile Steel (AH36) | |-----------|----------------------|-----------------------------| | (i) Min. yield strength | 235 N/mm² | 355 N/mm² | | (ii) Carbon content | $\leq 0.21\%$ | $\leq 0.18\%$ + micro-alloys (Nb, V) | | (ii) CEV | typically 0.35 – 0.40 | typically 0.38 – 0.45 | | (iii) Weldability | Excellent (no preheat for thin plates) | Good; preheat required for thicker plates (t > 25 mm) | | (iv) Cost per tonne | Baseline (lower) | 8 – 15 % more expensive | | (v) Applications | Superstructure, internal bulkheads, secondary hull | Bottom plating, sheer strake, deck plating, hatch coaming | | (vi) Specific strength | $\sigma_y/\rho = 235/7850 = 0.0299$ N·m/kg | $355/7850 = 0.0452$ N·m/kg | Specific strength ratio: $$ \dfrac{f_{s,HTS}}{f_{s,MS}} = \dfrac{0.0452}{0.0299} = 1.51 $$ Hence AH36 is **51 % stronger per unit weight** than mild steel. --- **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $t_{MS} = 22$ mm, $\sigma_{y,MS} = 235$ N/mm² - $\sigma_{y,HTS} = 355$ N/mm² (DH36) - Affected area $A = 1500$ m² - $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³ **Step 1 — Equivalent thickness:** $$ t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}} = 22 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{235}{355}} $$ From the previous numerical work, $\sqrt{235/355} = 0.8136$: $$ t_{HTS} = 22 \times 0.8136 = 17.90 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 2 — Reduction in thickness:** $$ \Delta t = 22 - 17.90 = 4.10 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 3 — Percentage reduction in weight:** *(3 marks)* $$ \%\text{ reduction} = \dfrac{\Delta t}{t_{MS}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4.10}{22} \times 100 = 18.64\% $$ (Remarkably, this is independent of area — the percentage is the same for any plate, as expected for a pure material substitution.) **Step 4 — Absolute weight saving (lightship reduction):** *(2 marks)* Mass of mild steel plate over 1500 m²: $$ M_{MS} = \rho \cdot A \cdot t_{MS} = 7850 \times 1500 \times 0.022 = 259{,}050 \text{ kg} = 259.05 \text{ tonnes} $$ Mass of DH36 plate over the same area: $$ M_{HTS} = 7850 \times 1500 \times 0.0179 = 210{,}772.5 \text{ kg} = 210.77 \text{ tonnes} $$ Weight saving (lightship reduction): $$ \Delta W = 259.05 - 210.77 = 48.28 \text{ tonnes} $$ **Step 5 — Deadweight gain:** Since lighter hull directly translates to additional cargo capacity (assuming the same displacement limit): $$ \text{Deadweight gain} = 48.28 \text{ tonnes (or metric tonnes)} $$ **Conclusion:** Substituting DH36 in the bottom-plating region reduces the lightship weight by approximately **48.3 tonnes** and increases the deadweight by the same amount — a significant commercial benefit for a bulk carrier. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Losing Pitfalls in HTS Questions (KTU Board Examiner Insights):** > > 1. **Linear instead of square-root thickness conversion** — Many students incorrectly write $t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \times \dfrac{\sigma_{y,MS}}{\sigma_{y,HTS}}$. This is **wrong**; it overestimates weight saving and loses up to **2 marks**. Always remember: **section modulus scales as $t^{2}$**, so thickness scales as $\sqrt{\sigma_{y,MS}/\sigma_{y,HTS}}$. > > 2. **Forgetting the round-up to standard plate size** — Calculated $t_{HTS} = 16.27$ mm is *not* a standard supply size. The student must specify the next standard size (e.g., 17 mm) and add corrosion + mill allowance. Failure to do so = **1-mark deduction**. > > 3. **Mixing up grade letter (A/D/E) with strength (32/36)** — Letter A, D, E refer to *toughness and delivery condition*, NOT to strength. A student writing "Grade A36" is treated as a serious conceptual error. > > 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — N/mm², kg/m², tonnes, m² — units are mandatory in KTU valuation. Missing units = **0.5 to 1 mark** lost. > > 5. **Forgetting to mention welding considerations** when recommending HTS — A complete KTU answer must reference **CEV, preheat temperature, and electrode selection** (e.g., basic low-hydrogen electrodes for DH/EH grades). > > 6. **Confusing AH/DH/EH with quenched & tempered vs TMCP** — Examiners specifically test whether you know that **E-grade is TMCP, D-grade is Q&T, and A-grade is normalised**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Higher Tensile Steel** - ☐ **Definition:** HTS = steel with $\sigma_y \geq 315$ N/mm², used in primary hull. - ☐ **Two strength grades:** 32 (315 MPa) and 36 (355 MPa). - ☐ **Three quality grades:** A ($0°$C, normalised), D ($-20°$C, Q&T), E ($-40°$C, TMCP). - ☐ **Common grades:** AH32, DH32, EH32, AH36, DH36, EH36. - ☐ **Master formula:** $t_{HTS} = t_{MS} \cdot \sqrt{\sigma_{y,MS} / \sigma_{y,HTS}}$. - ☐ **Typical weight saving:** **15 % to 25 %** in affected structure. - ☐ **Specific strength of AH36:** ~51 % higher than mild steel. - ☐ **Weldability check:** CEV $\leq 0.45$ (IIW formula). - ☐ **Key applications:** bottom plating, side shell (sheer strake), deck plating, hatch coaming, rudder horn, stem. - ☐ **Where NOT to use:** secondary superstructure, non-critical internal members. - ☐ **Always add:** corrosion allowance (0.5–1.0 mm) + mill tolerance (0.3–0.8 mm). - ☐ **Examiners love:** numerical problems on thickness reduction and weight saving. - ☐ **Pitfall:** square-root law, not linear — remember $t^{2}$ in section modulus. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Higher Tensile Steel: Properties and Application**

topics.title: Aluminium Alloys - Properties and Applications, Proof Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aluminium Alloys - Properties and Applications, Proof Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aluminium — The Marine Structural Metal

**Aluminium (Al)** is a silvery-white, soft, low-density metal with the chemical symbol **Al** and atomic number **13**. In its pure form, it possesses a tensile strength of only about **90 MPa**, which is far too low for any structural use. To make it suitable for engineering — and especially **naval architecture** — applications, it is alloyed with elements such as **copper (Cu), magnesium (Mg), manganese (Mn), silicon (Si), and zinc (Zn)**. The resulting **aluminium alloys** combine the light weight of pure aluminium with mechanical strength comparable to, and in some cases exceeding, that of mild steel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Aluminium alloys are metallic materials composed primarily of aluminium (≥ 90%) combined with one or more alloying elements to enhance mechanical strength, corrosion resistance, and workability for marine structural applications.*

The two most critical physical metrics that drive the use of aluminium in shipbuilding are:

| Metric | Pure Aluminium | Mild Steel (Reference) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Density** | **2700 kg/m³** | 7850 kg/m³ |
| **Young's Modulus (E)** | **70 GPa** | 210 GPa |
| **Specific Gravity** | **2.7** | 7.85 |

> [!NOTE]
> Aluminium is roughly **one-third the weight of steel** but has only **one-third the stiffness**. This trade-off is fundamental to ship design: weight savings reduce displacement and increase payload, but deflection control (using increased section depth or stiffeners) becomes a governing design criterion.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine two identical boats — one built from steel and one from aluminium alloy. If you place a **1 kg weight** on the steel boat, the hull may deflect by a small amount. Place that same **1 kg weight** on the aluminium boat, and it will deflect approximately **three times more**, because aluminium is three times less stiff. However, you can lift the aluminium boat's hull much more easily — it feels like lifting a **large piece of plywood**, while the steel hull feels like lifting a **dense hardwood plank**.

Now imagine the boat speeding through waves. The aluminium superstructure (the part above the main deck) is **lighter**, so the ship's **centre of gravity is lower** (since heavy machinery, engines, and cargo sit low in the hull), improving **stability**. At the same time, the lighter superstructure reduces the **overall vertical centre of gravity (KG)**, decreases the **righting lever (GZ)** demands at small angles, and most importantly — **reduces the ship's displacement for a given payload**, allowing either a higher payload or better fuel economy.

> [!TIP]
> **Why aluminium matters in ships:** Every **1000 kg** of weight saved in a superstructure roughly translates to a **1.5–2.0%** improvement in fuel efficiency over the operational life of a vessel. This is why cruise liners, naval frigates, and fast ferries extensively use aluminium for upper decks, masts, and bridge wings.

---

## 1.3 Proof Stress — The Critical Concept for Aluminium

Unlike **mild steel**, aluminium alloys **do not exhibit a sharply defined yield point** on their stress-strain diagram. There is no sudden "knee" or flat plateau. The material transitions **gradually** from elastic to plastic deformation. This makes the classical definition of yield stress (the stress at which plastic deformation begins) **impossible to apply directly**.

To overcome this, naval architects and materials engineers use a **proof stress** (specifically, the **0.2% proof stress**, denoted **Rp0.2**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition of Proof Stress (Rp0.2):**
> *The proof stress is the stress that produces a permanent (plastic) strain of a specified percentage (commonly 0.2%) of the original gauge length when the load is removed. For aluminium alloys, the 0.2% proof stress is universally adopted as the design yield stress.*

**The Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine stretching a rubber band slowly. If you stretch it by 1% and release, it snaps back — that is **elastic**. If you stretch it by 5% and release, it stays stretched by 0.5% — that small permanent stretch is the **proof strain**. The stress required to create that tiny permanent stretch is the **proof stress**. For aluminium, that tiny permanent stretch is defined as **0.2% (or 0.002 strain)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Aluminium Alloys

Aluminium alloys are classified along **two parallel systems** that students must internalize for KTU exams:

### A. By Mechanical Processing Route

1. **Wrought Alloys** — Mechanically worked (rolled, extruded, forged) into plates, sheets, extrusions, and forgings. Used for ship plates, stiffeners, superstructure panels.
2. **Cast Alloys** — Poured into moulds. Used for complex-shaped components like winch housings, valve bodies, and pumps.

### B. By Heat-Treatment Response

1. **Heat-Treatable Alloys** — Strengthened by **solution heat-treatment + quenching + artificial/natural aging**. Examples: **2xxx (Al-Cu)**, **6xxx (Al-Mg-Si)**, **7xxx (Al-Zn)** series.
2. **Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys** — Strengthened by **cold working (strain hardening)** alone. Examples: **3xxx (Al-Mn)**, **5xxx (Al-Mg)** series.

> [!NOTE]
> **For shipbuilding, the 5xxx and 6xxx series dominate:**
> - **5xxx series (Al-Mg)** — Excellent corrosion resistance in seawater, used for hull plates of small craft and patrol boats.
> - **6xxx series (Al-Mg-Si)** — Good strength, excellent extrudability, used for superstructure profiles, masts, and bridge structures.

### 2.1.1 Common Marine Aluminium Alloys — KTU Must-Know Table

| Alloy | Series | Type | Rp0.2 (MPa) | Rm (MPa) | Marine Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **5083** | 5xxx | Non-heat-treatable | **125** | 275 | Hull plates, LNG carriers |
| **5086** | 5xxx | Non-heat-treatable | **115** | 260 | Hull plates, boat hulls |
| **5454** | 5xxx | Non-heat-treatable | **115** | 250 | Pressure vessels, pipework |
| **5456** | 5xxx | Non-heat-treatable | **130** | 285 | High-strength hull plating |
| **6061** | 6xxx | Heat-treatable | **240** | 310 | Superstructures, masts |
| **6082** | 6xxx | Heat-treatable | **260** | 340 | Heavy structural extrusions |

---

## 2.2 Key Properties of Aluminium Alloys Relevant to Ship Structures

The following properties govern the use of aluminium in marine design:

- **Low Density (2700 kg/m³):** Reduces displacement, increases payload.
- **High Strength-to-Weight Ratio:** For 5083-H116, σ/ρ ≈ **45 kNm/kg** vs mild steel ≈ **30 kNm/kg**.
- **Excellent Corrosion Resistance:** Self-passivating oxide layer (Al₂O₃) protects against seawater.
- **Good Weldability:** 5xxx series welds without losing corrosion resistance.
- **Non-Magnetic:** Useful for mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs).
- **Non-Sparking:** Safer for fuel tankers and ammunition ships.
- **Low Modulus of Elasticity (70 GPa):** Increases deflection — requires deeper sections.
- **Low Fatigue Endurance Compared to Steel:** Critical for cyclically loaded hulls.
- **Good Low-Temperature Toughness:** Retains ductility at cryogenic temperatures (LNG service).

---

## 2.3 Proof Stress — The Underlying Theory

Since aluminium alloys show a **continuous, gradual transition** from elastic to plastic behaviour, the stress-strain curve is non-linear from the very beginning of plastic flow. Engineers therefore use the **Offset Method** to define a practical yield criterion.

The **0.2% Proof Stress (Rp0.2)** is found by:

1. Drawing the **stress-strain curve** from the origin.
2. Drawing a line **parallel to the initial linear (elastic) portion** of the curve.
3. Offsetting this line along the strain axis by an amount equal to **0.2% of the gauge length (i.e., 0.002 strain)**.
4. The stress at which this offset line **intersects** the stress-strain curve is **Rp0.2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** This 0.2% permanent strain is considered the maximum tolerable deformation in a ship structure without compromising function. Beyond Rp0.2, the structure undergoes progressive plastic deformation leading to collapse.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Formula | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\sigma$ | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | MPa (N/mm²) |
| $\varepsilon$ | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | dimensionless |
| $E$ | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ (elastic region) | GPa |
| $R_{p0.2}$ | 0.2% Proof Stress | Stress at $\varepsilon_{plastic} = 0.002$ | MPa |
| $R_m$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength | Max stress on curve | MPa |
| $A\%$ | Percentage Elongation | $A\% = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % |
| $\rho$ | Density (Aluminium) | $\rho_{Al} = \mathbf{2700 \ kg/m^3}$ | kg/m³ |
| $\sigma_{allow}$ | Allowable Stress (Classification Societies) | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{R_{p0.2}}{\text{Safety Factor (SF)}}$ | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Society Safety Factors for Aluminium (Typical):**
> - **Hull girder bending:** SF = 1.5 to 1.67
> - **Local plating (yielding):** SF = 1.5
> - **Buckling:** SF = 1.5 to 2.0
> - **Fatigue:** Based on S-N curves and design life (typically 25 years for commercial vessels)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **ship classification rules** (DNV, Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS), the **0.2% proof stress Rp0.2** is the **single most important material property** used in aluminium hull and superstructure scantling calculations. All section modulus (Z) requirements, plate thickness (t) formulae, and stiffener spacing rules are built directly around Rp0.2. The classification societies publish **minimum Rp0.2 values** for each alloy-temper combination (e.g., 5083-O: Rp0.2 ≥ 125 MPa; 6082-T6: Rp0.2 ≥ 260 MPa) and design must ensure that **induced stress ≤ Rp0.2 / Safety Factor**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Determination of 0.2% Proof Stress from Stress-Strain Data

The offset method requires a systematic calculation. Below is the **exhaustive analytical procedure** that students must master for KTU 14-mark derivations.

### 3.1.1 Given Information

A specimen of aluminium alloy **6082-T6** is tested in tension. The original gauge length is **L₀ = 50 mm**, and the original cross-sectional area is **A₀ = 78.5 mm²** (a 10 mm diameter round bar).

### 3.1.2 Step 1: Convert Load-Extension Data to Stress-Strain Data

For each recorded load (F) and corresponding extension (ΔL):

$$
\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{F}{78.5} \quad \text{(in MPa when F is in N)}
$$

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{50} \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
$$

This step is the **foundation** of the analysis. **Every data point** in the load-extension table must be converted to a corresponding point on the stress-strain diagram before any graphical construction is performed.

### 3.1.3 Step 2: Plot the Stress-Strain Curve

Plot $\sigma$ on the **y-axis** (ordinate) and $\varepsilon$ on the **x-axis** (abscissa). For 6082-T6, the curve will be **smooth, with no distinct yield point** — a typical aluminium response.

### 3.1.4 Step 3: Identify the Initial Linear Elastic Region

From the origin, the first few points of the curve lie on a **straight line**. The slope of this line gives the **Young's Modulus**:

$$
E = \frac{\sigma_{elastic}}{\varepsilon_{elastic}}
$$

For 6082-T6, E ≈ **70 GPa**. Identify at least two points in this region, e.g., ($\varepsilon_1 = 0.001$, $\sigma_1 = 70$ MPa) and ($\varepsilon_2 = 0.002$, $\sigma_2 = 140$ MPa).

### 3.1.5 Step 4: Construct the 0.2% Offset Line

Draw a line **parallel to the initial linear elastic region** (i.e., having slope = E), but offset along the **strain axis** by:

$$
\varepsilon_{offset} = \frac{0.2}{100} = 0.002
$$

This means the offset line starts at the point $(\varepsilon = 0.002, \sigma = 0)$ on the strain axis, not at the origin.

### 3.1.6 Step 5: Mathematical Equation of the Offset Line

The offset line has the same slope as the elastic line, which is **E**. Its equation, in stress-strain coordinates, is:

$$
\sigma_{offset} = E \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002)
$$

In our example, with E = 70,000 MPa:

$$
\sigma_{offset} = 70000 \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002)
$$

This is the **key analytical tool** — the offset line is a straight line that intercepts the strain axis at $\varepsilon = 0.002$.

### 3.1.7 Step 6: Find the Intersection with the Actual Curve

The 0.2% proof stress is the stress at which the offset line **intersects** the experimentally measured stress-strain curve. In tabular terms, scan through the data and find the row where:

- The calculated offset-line stress $\sigma_{offset}$
- and the measured stress $\sigma_{measured}$
- differ by less than the smallest tabular interval.

### 3.1.8 Worked Numerical Example

Consider the following **test data** for 6082-T6 (gauge length 50 mm, area 78.5 mm²):

| Load F (kN) | Extension ΔL (mm) | Stress $\sigma$ (MPa) | Strain $\varepsilon$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | 0.000 | 0 | 0.0000 |
| 5.5 | 0.027 | 70 | 0.0005 |
| 11.0 | 0.054 | 140 | 0.0011 |
| 16.5 | 0.082 | 210 | 0.0016 |
| 22.0 | 0.112 | 280 | 0.0022 |
| 27.5 | 0.155 | 350 | 0.0031 |
| 33.0 | 0.225 | 420 | 0.0045 |
| 38.5 | 0.350 | 490 | 0.0070 |
| 44.0 | 0.600 | 560 | 0.0120 |

**Calculate the offset line stress at each strain point:**

$$
\sigma_{offset} = 70000 \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002)
$$

| Strain $\varepsilon$ | $\sigma_{offset}$ (MPa) | $\sigma_{measured}$ (MPa) | Comparison |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.0016 | $70000 \cdot (0.0016 - 0.002) = -28$ | 210 | Curve **above** offset |
| 0.0022 | $70000 \cdot (0.0022 - 0.002) = 14$ | 280 | Curve **above** offset |
| 0.0031 | $70000 \cdot (0.0031 - 0.002) = 77$ | 350 | Curve **above** offset |
| 0.0045 | $70000 \cdot (0.0045 - 0.002) = 175$ | 420 | Curve **above** offset |
| 0.0070 | $70000 \cdot (0.0070 - 0.002) = 350$ | 490 | Curve **above** offset |

> [!NOTE]
> The intersection occurs **between** $\varepsilon = 0.0070$ and the next data point. The proof stress Rp0.2 lies between 350 MPa and 490 MPa. For a more precise answer, one may use **linear interpolation** between the two bracketing data points, or perform a **least-squares fit** of the plastic region.

The standard published value of **Rp0.2 for 6082-T6 is approximately 260–280 MPa**, which corresponds to the **yielding region** in the real curve (the offset line crosses the curve in the knee where plastic flow begins). For exam purposes, the value is read **directly from the intersection** of the offset line and the stress-strain curve.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Allowable Stress for an Aluminium Hull Plate

Given a plate of alloy **5083-H116** with Rp0.2 = 125 MPa, classification society safety factor SF = 1.5:

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{R_{p0.2}}{SF} = \frac{125}{1.5}
$$

$$
\sigma_{allow} = 83.33 \ MPa
$$

This allowable stress is then used in plate bending theory to compute the **required thickness**:

$$
t_{req} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \cdot M}{\sigma_{allow} \cdot 1}} = \sqrt{6M / \sigma_{allow}}
$$

where M is the bending moment per unit width (N·mm/mm).

> [!TIP]
> The lower Rp0.2 of aluminium alloys (compared to mild steel at 235 MPa) means that **aluminium plates are often thicker** than equivalent steel plates for the same loading — partially offsetting the weight advantage in plate applications. This is why aluminium is most advantageous in **superstructures and upper decks**, where weight reduction of the upper part of the ship significantly reduces the **vertical moment of inertia** of the hull girler and lowers the required scantlings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Aluminium Alloy Classification for Marine Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aluminium Alloys] --> B[Wrought Alloys]
    A --> C[Cast Alloys]
    B --> D[Heat Treatable]
    B --> E[Non Heat Treatable]
    D --> F[2xxx Al Cu]
    D --> G[6xxx Al Mg Si]
    D --> H[7xxx Al Zn]
    E --> I[3xxx Al Mn]
    E --> J[5xxx Al Mg]
    F --> K[Aerospace Marine Fittings]
    G --> L[6082 T6 Superstructure Masts]
    G --> M[6061 T6 Bridge Structures]
    J --> N[5083 H116 Hull Plates]
    J --> O[5086 O Boat Hulls]
    J --> P[5456 H321 High Strength Hull]
    C --> Q[Winch Housings Valve Bodies]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Proof Stress Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Tensile Test] --> B[Record Load and Extension]
    B --> C[Convert to Stress and Strain]
    C --> D[Plot Stress Strain Curve]
    D --> E[Identify Linear Elastic Region]
    E --> F[Calculate Modulus E]
    F --> G[Draw Offset Line Slope E at Strain 0.002]
    G --> H[Find Intersection with Curve]
    H --> I[Read Proof Stress Rp0.2 from Y axis]
    I --> J[Apply Safety Factor for Allowable Stress]
    J --> K[Use in Plate Stiffener Scantling]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Comparison: Mild Steel vs Aluminium Alloy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mild Steel] --> A1[Density 7850 kg per m3]
    A --> A2[Modulus 210 GPa]
    A --> A3[Sharp Yield Point at 235 MPa]
    A --> A4[Yield Stress used directly]
    B[Aluminium Alloy] --> B1[Density 2700 kg per m3]
    B --> B2[Modulus 70 GPa]
    B --> B3[No Sharp Yield Point]
    B --> B4[0.2 percent Proof Stress Rp0.2 used]
    A4 --> C[Design Comparison]
    B4 --> C
    C --> D[Aluminium needs deeper sections]
    C --> E[Aluminium gives 50 percent weight saving]
    C --> F[Aluminium resists corrosion better]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU exams:** A common 14-mark question asks students to "compare the stress-strain behaviour of mild steel and aluminium alloy and explain the concept of proof stress." The above block diagram is the **conceptual scaffold** of that answer. Use it to structure your exam response with a clear comparative table or pointwise argument.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Define proof stress. Why is it used in place of yield stress for aluminium alloys?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Proof stress is defined as the stress which produces a specified small permanent plastic strain, most commonly **0.2%** of the original gauge length, after unloading. It is denoted as **Rp0.2** and is read from the stress-strain curve using the offset method, where a line parallel to the initial elastic portion is drawn at a strain offset of **0.002**.

**Why used for aluminium (1.5 marks):** Aluminium alloys do not exhibit a clearly defined yield point on their stress-strain curve. The transition from elastic to plastic deformation is gradual and continuous. Therefore, the conventional yield stress cannot be identified. The 0.2% proof stress provides a **standardised, reproducible design value** that is adopted universally by classification societies and design codes for aluminium ship structures.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**List any three aluminium alloys used in shipbuilding and give one application for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Aluminium Alloy 5083 (Al-Mg-Mn series):** Used for **hull plates** of fast patrol boats, mine countermeasure vessels, and LNG carrier secondary barriers. (1 mark)
2. **Aluminium Alloy 6061 (Al-Mg-Si series):** Used for **superstructure panels, masts, and bridge wings** of commercial vessels due to good extrudability and weldability. (1 mark)
3. **Aluminium Alloy 6082 (Al-Mg-Si series):** Used for **heavy structural extrusions, deck fittings, and longitudinal stiffeners** in high-speed craft. (1 mark)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

#### (a) Explain the properties of aluminium alloys that make them suitable for marine applications. Discuss at least **six** important properties. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The following six properties make aluminium alloys exceptionally well suited for marine structural applications:

**1. Low Density (2700 kg/m³) (1 mark):** Aluminium is approximately one-third the weight of steel. This results in significant **weight reduction** in superstructures, hulls of small craft, and upper decks. A lighter vessel has lower displacement for the same payload, leading to **better fuel efficiency, higher speed, or increased cargo capacity**. The reduction in weight also lowers the ship's vertical centre of gravity (KG), improving stability.

**2. High Strength-to-Weight Ratio (1 mark):** Alloys such as 5083-H116 and 6082-T6 have strength-to-weight ratios that **exceed those of mild steel**. The 6082-T6 alloy has Rp0.2 = 260 MPa, and a specific strength of 260/2.7 ≈ 96 kNm/kg, far higher than mild steel's 235/7.85 ≈ 30 kNm/kg. This enables the construction of **lightweight yet strong** marine structures.

**3. Excellent Corrosion Resistance in Seawater (1.5 marks):** Aluminium spontaneously forms a thin, tenacious, self-healing oxide layer (Al₂O₃) on its surface. This layer prevents further oxidation and provides **outstanding resistance to corrosion** in marine atmospheres and seawater. The 5xxx series alloys are particularly noted for their seawater corrosion resistance, eliminating the need for heavy protective coatings in many applications.

**4. Good Weldability (1 mark):** The 5xxx series (especially 5083 and 5086) can be readily welded using **GTAW (TIG) and GMAW (MIG)** processes without significant loss of mechanical properties in the heat-affected zone. This is essential for fabricating large hull plates and superstructure sections.

**5. Non-Magnetic Behaviour (1 mark):** Aluminium is non-magnetic, which makes it ideal for **mine countermeasure vessels (MCMVs)** and degaussing-sensitive naval ships. The non-magnetic hull reduces the vessel's magnetic signature, lowering the risk of triggering magnetic sea mines.

**6. Non-Sparking and Low-Temperature Toughness (1.5 marks):** Aluminium alloys do not produce sparks on impact, making them safe for use in **fuel tankers, ammunition ships, and LNG carriers**. Additionally, they retain their ductility and toughness at **cryogenic temperatures**, making them suitable for LNG containment systems and polar vessel applications.

---

#### (b) Define proof stress and explain the offset method for its determination with a neat sketch. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** The 0.2% proof stress (Rp0.2) is defined as the stress which, when the load is completely removed, leaves a permanent (plastic) strain of **0.2%** of the original gauge length. Mathematically, it is the stress corresponding to a total strain such that the elastic (recoverable) portion is **0.2% less** than the total strain.

**Offset Method (Step-by-Step) (4 marks):**

**Step 1:** Conduct a standard tensile test on the aluminium specimen, recording load and extension data.

**Step 2:** Convert load to stress using $\sigma = F/A_0$ and extension to strain using $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$. Plot the **stress-strain curve**.

**Step 3:** Identify the **initial linear elastic portion** of the curve. The slope of this line gives the Young's Modulus E.

**Step 4:** Draw an **offset line** that is parallel to the initial elastic line, but **shifted to the right along the strain axis by 0.002** (i.e., 0.2%).

**Step 5:** The stress at which this offset line intersects the actual stress-strain curve is the **0.2% proof stress, Rp0.2**.

**Neat Sketch Description (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Y-axis:} \quad & \sigma \ (\text{stress, MPa}) \\
\text{X-axis:} \quad & \varepsilon \ (\text{strain}) \\
\text{Curve:} \quad & \text{Smooth, no yield point — typical aluminium behaviour} \\
\text{Offset line:} \quad & \text{Starts at } \varepsilon = 0.002 \text{ on x-axis, slope} = E \\
\text{Rp0.2:} \quad & \text{Y-coordinate of intersection point}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often draw the offset line starting from the **origin** instead of from $\varepsilon = 0.002$ on the strain axis. This is **WRONG** and will result in **loss of 1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing proof stress with **ultimate tensile strength (Rm)**. Rp0.2 is read at the **first yielding point**, while Rm is the **maximum stress** on the curve. They are **different quantities**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to **convert load to stress** before drawing the offset line. The offset must be applied on the **strain axis**, not the extension axis.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

#### (a) Classify aluminium alloys used in shipbuilding. Explain the difference between heat-treatable and non-heat-treatable alloys with examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Classification (3 marks):**

Aluminium alloys used in marine applications are classified along two main systems:

**(i) By Processing Route:**
- **Wrought Alloys:** Mechanically worked into plates, sheets, extrusions, and forgings. Dominant in shipbuilding.
- **Cast Alloys:** Molten metal poured into moulds. Used for complex shapes like winch housings.

**(ii) By Heat-Treatment Response:**
- **Heat-Treatable Alloys:** Strengthened by solution heat treatment, quenching, and aging.
- **Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys:** Strengthened by cold working (strain hardening) alone.

**Heat-Treatable Alloys (2 marks):**

These alloys achieve their strength through a controlled **three-stage process**:
1. **Solution Treatment:** Heating the alloy to ~500°C to dissolve alloying elements into a single-phase solid solution.
2. **Quenching:** Rapid cooling (usually in water) to retain the alloying elements in a supersaturated solid solution at room temperature.
3. **Aging:** Holding at an elevated temperature (artificial aging) or at room temperature (natural aging) to allow fine precipitates to form, which impede dislocation motion and increase strength.

**Examples:** 2xxx series (Al-Cu) — used for marine fittings; 6xxx series (Al-Mg-Si) — **6061-T6, 6082-T6** used for superstructures; 7xxx series (Al-Zn) — used in high-performance racing yachts.

**Non-Heat-Treatable Alloys (2 marks):**

These alloys cannot be strengthened by heat treatment because their alloying elements have very low solubility changes with temperature. Their strength is increased by **cold working** (rolling, drawing, or stretching at room temperature), which introduces dislocations and refines the grain structure.

**Examples:** 3xxx series (Al-Mn); **5xxx series (Al-Mg) — 5083, 5086, 5454, 5456** are the **workhorses of marine aluminium shipbuilding**, used for hull plates and structural components because they combine excellent corrosion resistance with good weldability.

---

#### (b) Compare the stress-strain behaviour of mild steel and aluminium alloy. Why is the design of aluminium structures different from steel structures? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Mild Steel | Aluminium Alloy (e.g., 6082-T6) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Density | 7850 kg/m³ | 2700 kg/m³ |
| Young's Modulus | 210 GPa | 70 GPa |
| Stress-Strain Curve Shape | Linear up to a **distinct yield point**, then flat plateau (perfectly plastic region), followed by strain hardening | Smooth, **gradually curving** from origin with no yield point |
| Yield Criterion | **Yield Stress** (σy ≈ 235 MPa for mild steel) read directly at the knee | **0.2% Proof Stress (Rp0.2)** determined by offset method |
| Ultimate Tensile Strength | 410–510 MPa | 270–340 MPa |
| Elongation at Fracture | 20–30% | 10–15% |
| Strain at Failure | High (ductile) | Moderate |

**Differences in Design Approach (4 marks):**

**1. Yield Criterion:** Mild steel has a **clearly defined yield point** marked by a sudden load drop or plateau, allowing direct identification of yield stress. Aluminium alloys have a **continuous stress-strain curve** with no yield point, requiring the **0.2% proof stress** convention.

**2. Deflection Control:** Since E for aluminium is **one-third** that of steel, aluminium structures deflect **three times more** under the same load. To control deflection, designers must either **increase the section modulus** (deeper sections) or **reduce the unsupported span**. This is a critical design consideration in superstructure design where stiffness governs the scantlings.

**3. Plate Buckling:** Aluminium plates are more susceptible to **buckling** due to lower E. Buckling strength is proportional to $\sqrt{E}$, so an aluminium plate buckles at approximately $\sqrt{70/210} \approx 0.58$ times the stress of an equivalent steel plate. Stiffener spacing must be reduced or plate thickness increased to prevent buckling.

**4. Fatigue Performance:** Aluminium alloys have **lower fatigue endurance** compared to steel, with endurance limits typically 30–40% of Rp0.2 (vs 50–55% for steel). Fatigue becomes a governing criterion in cyclically loaded hull plating.

**5. Welding and Connections:** Aluminium welding requires **special techniques** (GTAW, GMAW with AC), inert gas shielding (argon), and the use of **appropriate filler alloys** (e.g., 5356, 4043). Bolted connections may suffer from **galvanic corrosion** if steel fasteners are used — they must be isolated with non-conductive gaskets or use aluminium/stainless steel fasteners with proper insulation.

**6. Corrosion Protection:** Steel hulls require **extensive coating systems** (anti-corrosive + anti-fouling) and cathodic protection. Aluminium hulls of 5xxx series are largely **self-protecting** due to the oxide layer, though they still require cathodic protection in some cases to prevent galvanic corrosion from bronze propellers or steel fittings.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Aluminium Density:** $\rho = 2700 \ kg/m^3$ (one-third of steel).
- **Aluminium Young's Modulus:** $E = 70 \ GPa$ (one-third of steel).
- **Most common marine alloys:** 5083 (hull), 6082 (superstructure), 6061 (general), 5456 (high strength).
- **5xxx series = Al-Mg = non-heat-treatable = excellent seawater corrosion resistance.**
- **6xxx series = Al-Mg-Si = heat-treatable (T6 temper) = high strength for extrusions.**
- **Proof Stress Definition:** Stress producing **0.2% permanent strain** after unloading.
- **Notation:** $R_{p0.2}$ — always specify the offset (0.2% is standard for aluminium).
- **Offset Method:** Draw line parallel to elastic region, shifted by **0.002 strain**, intersect with curve.
- **No yield point in aluminium** — proof stress replaces yield stress in design.
- **Allowable stress formula:** $\sigma_{allow} = R_{p0.2} / SF$ where SF is from classification society rules.
- **Aluminium is non-magnetic** — used in MCMVs.
- **Aluminium is non-sparking** — used in fuel tankers and ammunition ships.
- **Aluminium retains toughness at low temperatures** — used in LNG carriers.
- **Galvanic corrosion** is a critical design issue when aluminium is connected to steel or copper alloys.
- **Aluminium welds using TIG/GMAW** with **AC current** (for oxide layer cleaning) and **argon shielding**.
- **Specific strength** of aluminium (Rp0.2 / ρ) is often **2–3 times** that of mild steel.
- **Plate buckling** is a major concern in aluminium hulls — stiffer spacing or thicker plates are required.
- **Elongation at fracture** for aluminium is **10–15%**, lower than mild steel's **20–30%**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Metallic Materials - GRP and FRP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Non-Metallic Materials - GRP and FRP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Non-Metallic Materials in Shipbuilding

The modern shipbuilding industry has progressively shifted from a purely steel-dominated construction paradigm to a **multi-material design philosophy**. Among the most significant non-metallic structural materials, **Fiber Reinforced Polymers (FRP)** — and their most common subset, **Glass Reinforced Plastics (GRP)** — occupy a position of paramount importance. These materials combine the high tensile strength of reinforcing fibers with the corrosion resistance, formability, and lightweight characteristics of polymer matrices to deliver a superior strength-to-weight ratio that is critical for high-speed vessels, naval auxiliaries, pleasure craft, and offshore platforms.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The treatment of GRP/FRP under *Ship Structures I* focuses on the **constituent materials**, **laminate construction principles**, **mechanical properties relevant to scantling**, and the **classification society rules (DNV, Lloyd's, IRS) governing their use** in primary and secondary ship structures.

---

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition

**Glass Reinforced Plastic (GRP)** is a **fibre-reinforced polymer composite** in which the reinforcement phase consists predominantly of **glass fibres** (typically E-glass due to its favourable cost-performance profile) embedded within a **thermosetting polymer matrix** (most commonly unsaturated polyester, vinyl ester, or epoxy resin). The fibres carry the **tensile and flexural loads**, while the matrix transfers stress between fibres, protects them from environmental degradation, and provides the geometric stability of the finished laminate.

**Fibre Reinforced Plastic (FRP)** is the **broader, generic classification** that encompasses GRP and additionally includes composites reinforced with **carbon fibres (CFRP)**, **aramid fibres (AFRP)**, and **boron fibres (BFRP)**, often in hybrid configurations. In the context of KTU naval architecture coursework, the term FRP is used generically to describe any structural composite in which continuous or discontinuous fibres provide the principal load-bearing capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definitional Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - **GRP** ⊂ **FRP** (i.e., every GRP is an FRP, but not every FRP is a GRP).
> - GRP is the **specific case** where the reinforcement is glass; FRP is the **generic family** of all fibre-reinforced polymers.
> - In shipbuilding vernacular, "GRP" and "Fibreglass" are used interchangeably; "FRP" is the technically precise term used in classification society rules.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (The "Concrete & Rebar" Concept)

Imagine constructing a column out of **pure cement**. The cement alone is **strong in compression** (you can stack heavy weights on it) but **brittle and weak in tension** (it cracks easily if you try to bend it). This is analogous to a **neat polymer resin** — it has reasonable compressive strength and good chemical resistance, but it is too brittle and weak in tension to be used as a primary structural material on its own.

Now imagine **embedding steel reinforcing bars (rebar)** into that cement. The **cement** (now playing the role of the **polymer matrix**) holds the bars in place, transfers the load between them, and protects them from corrosion. The **steel rebar** (playing the role of the **glass/carbon fibre**) carries the tensile and flexural loads with very high efficiency. The resulting **reinforced concrete** is dramatically stronger in tension, flexure, and impact than either constituent alone.

**GRP/FRP works on exactly this same principle:**
- The **fibres** are the "rebar" — they are the load-bearing skeleton.
- The **resin/matrix** is the "concrete" — it holds the fibres together, distributes stress, and provides shape and surface finish.
- The **composite** (fibre + matrix) has **properties superior to either constituent alone**.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy is powerful for naval architects:** Just as the civil engineer would never build a high-rise building using only the "rebar" or only the "concrete," the ship designer must always think of GRP/FRP as an **integrated composite system** — selecting the fibre type, fibre volume fraction, fibre orientation, and matrix resin *jointly* to meet the structural and environmental demands of the marine service condition.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Performance Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally referenced in classification society rules (Lloyd's Register, DNV, IRS) and academic literature for GRP/FRP marine composites:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Marine GRP) | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of E-glass fibre | $\rho_f$ | **2550 – 2600 kg/m³** | ISO 1172 |
| Density of polyester resin | $\rho_m$ | **1100 – 1250 kg/m³** | Manufacturer datasheet |
| Density of cured GRP laminate | $\rho_c$ | **1500 – 1800 kg/m³** | Lloyd's Register Rules |
| Density of CFRP laminate | $\rho_c$ | **1500 – 1600 kg/m³** | ASTM D792 |
| Young's Modulus, E-glass fibre | $E_f$ | **70 – 80 GPa** | ASTM D2343 |
| Young's Modulus, polyester resin | $E_m$ | **2.5 – 4.0 GPa** | ASTM D638 |
| Young's Modulus, cured GRP (unidirectional) | $E_{1L}$ | **25 – 45 GPa** | ASTM D3039 |
| Young's Modulus, cured GRP (mat/woven) | $E$ | **7 – 15 GPa** | ASTM D3039 |
