# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**State the governing differential equation of a thin elastic plate and list the four boundary conditions for a plate simply supported on all four edges.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The plate equation is

$$D\left(\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}}\right) = q(x,y)$$

where D = E h³ / [12(1−ν²)] is the flexural rigidity (½ mark).

For SSSS, four boundary conditions are:

1. **w = 0 at x = 0, a** (geometric — no deflection) (½ mark)
2. **M_x = 0 at x = 0, a** → ∂²w/∂x² = 0 (natural — no moment) (½ mark)
3. **w = 0 at y = 0, b** (½ mark)
4. **M_y = 0 at y = 0, b** → ∂²w/∂y² = 0 (½ mark)

[Total: 3 marks — ½ mark each for the PDE form, the meaning of D, and the four BCs]

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Why is Navier's solution restricted to rectangular plates with all four edges simply supported? Justify with reference to the assumed form of the deflection series.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Navier assumes the deflection as a double sine series: $w = \sum\sum w_{mn}\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b)$ (1 mark). The sine function **naturally satisfies** the SSSS conditions:
- sin(0) = sin(mπ) = 0  ⇒  w = 0 on x = 0, a (½ mark)
- d²/dx²[sin(mπx/a)] = −(mπ/a)² sin(mπx/a) = 0 on x = 0, a  ⇒  M_x = 0 (½ mark)
- and similarly for the y-direction (½ mark).

For any other boundary type (clamped, free, or mixed), the sine function **does not** satisfy the BCs, and a different trial function (e.g., Levy beams, or FEM) must be used (½ mark). Hence, the Navier solution is **structurally limited** to the SSSS case.

[Total: 3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Note on choice pattern (KTU 2024):** Each Part B carries 14 marks, broken into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer EITHER the whole Q-A or the whole Q-B.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model]

**A rectangular steel plate of size a = 1.2 m × b = 0.8 m, thickness h = 8 mm, is simply supported on all four edges and carries a uniformly distributed load q₀ = 40 kN/m². Take E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.30.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Derive the Navier solution for the deflection of a SSSS rectangular plate under UDL. Show that the deflection series is**
$$w(x,y) = \frac{16 q_{0}}{\pi^{6} D} \sum_{m=1,3,5\ldots}\sum_{n=1,3,5\ldots} \frac{\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b)}{m n \left[(m/a)^{2}+(n/b)^{2}\right]^{2}}$$

**Model Solution:**

1. Assume double sine series $w = \sum\sum w_{mn}\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b)$ (1 mark)
2. Expand UDL: $q_{mn} = (4q_0/ab)\int_0^a\int_0^b \sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b)\,dx\,dy$ (1 mark)
3. Evaluate the integrals: only odd m, n contribute, giving $q_{mn} = 16q_0/(\pi^2 mn)$ (2 marks)
4. Substitute in plate equation to obtain $w_{mn} = q_{mn}/\left\{\pi^4 D[(m/a)^2+(n/b)^2]^2\right\}$ (2 marks)
5. Substitute back: $w = (16q_0/\pi^6 D) \sum\sum \sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b) / \left\{mn[(m/a)^2+(n/b)^2]^2\right\}$ over odd m, n (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the maximum deflection at the centre of the plate using the first three (m, n) terms of the series.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Flexural rigidity:**
   $D = Eh^3/[12(1−ν^2)] = (2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.008)^3/(12 \times 0.91) = 9.374 \times 10^3$ N·m (1 mark)

2. **Compute first three w_mn at centre** (x = a/2, y = b/2): signs alternate by (−1)^((m+n−2)/2) (1 mark)

   - m=1, n=1: $w_{11} = \frac{16\times 40{,}000}{\pi^6 \times 9374 \times 1 \times 1 \times [(1/1.2)^2+(1/0.8)^2]^2} = \frac{6.4\times 10^5}{9374 \times 900.84\,\pi^4} = 1.962 \times 10^{-3}$ m (1.5 marks)
   - m=1, n=3: contribution = $-0.2116 \times 10^{-3}$ m (1.5 marks)
   - m=3, n=1: contribution = $-0.3809 \times 10^{-3}$ m (1.5 marks)
   - All higher terms: ≈ −0.01 × 10⁻³ m (negligible) (½ mark)

3. **Total:** $w_{max} \approx 1.962 - 0.212 - 0.381 = 1.369 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **1.37 mm** (1 mark)

4. **Compare with first-term approximation:** 1.962 mm → series converges within 30 % even with first correction; 3 terms give excellent accuracy (½ mark)

[Total: 7 marks — 1 + 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 + ½ + 1 + ½ = 7]

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model]

**A square steel plate of side a = 1.0 m, thickness h = 12 mm, is simply supported on all four edges and carries a concentrated load P = 25 kN at the centre. Material: E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa, ν = 0.30.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Using Navier's method, derive the closed-form expression for the deflection coefficient w_mn when a concentrated load P acts at the centre of the plate.**

**Model Solution:**

1. The concentrated load at (a/2, b/2) is q(x, y) = P δ(x − a/2) δ(y − b/2) (½ mark)
2. Substitute into the q_mn integral:
   $q_{mn} = (4P/ab)\sin(m\pi/2)\sin(n\pi/2)$ (1 mark)
3. For odd m, n, the sign is (−1)^((m+n−2)/2); for even m or n, sin = 0, so q_mn = 0 (1 mark)
4. Hence the surviving modes have **odd m, n** only, with $q_{mn} = (4P/a^2)(-1)^{(m+n-2)/2}$ (1 mark)
5. From the master Navier result, $w_{mn} = q_{mn}/\{\pi^4 D[(m/a)^2+(n/b)^2]^2\}$ (1 mark)
6. For square plate a = b: $w_{mn} = (4P/(\pi^4 D a^2)) \cdot (-1)^{(m+n-2)/2}/(m^2+n^2)^2$ (1.5 marks)
7. Final deflection:
   $$w(x,y) = \frac{4P}{\pi^4 D a^2} \sum_{m,n\,\text{odd}} \frac{(-1)^{(m+n-2)/2}\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b)}{(m^2+n^2)^2}$$ (1 mark)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compute the maximum deflection at the plate centre using the (1, 1) mode alone and compare with the converged value.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Flexural rigidity:**
   $D = (2.0 \times 10^{11})(0.012)^3/(12 \times 0.91) = 3.165 \times 10^4$ N·m (1 mark)

2. **First-mode (m = n = 1) deflection at centre** (x = a/2, y = a/2):
   $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$ and sign $(−1)^0 = +1$, so
   $w_{11} = (4 \times 25{,}000)/(\pi^4 \times 3.165 \times 10^4 \times 1.0^2) = 1.0 \times 10^5/(3.084 \times 10^6) = 0.0324$ m
   = **32.4 mm** (3 marks)

3. **Converged value** (sum over odd m, n up to m = n = 7): theoretical result is
   $w_{max} \approx 0.00560 P a^2/D = 0.00560 \times 25{,}000 \times 1.0^2/(3.165 \times 10^4) = 4.42 \times 10^{-3}$ m = **4.42 mm** (2 marks)

4. **Comparison:** The first-mode answer (32.4 mm) **over-estimates by a factor of ~7.3**. This is because the concentrated load excites high-frequency modes much more strongly than a UDL. Therefore, **a first-mode truncation is NOT acceptable for concentrated loads** — many terms are needed (1 mark).

[Total: 7 marks]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls costing marks in Navier-solution questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting (1 − ν²) in D** — a 1-mark deduction *every time* it is omitted in a calculation.
> 2. **Using the wrong Fourier series** (cosine instead of sine) for the SSSS case — the BCs are not satisfied; result is mathematically wrong.
> 3. **Treating m, n as continuous** when summing the series — they are *positive integers*.
> 4. **Forgetting the "odd-m, n" filter for UDL** — including even-m or even-n terms in q_mn gives zero contribution but wastes time.
> 5. **Sign error at the centre**: missing the factor (−1)^((m+n−2)/2) — w_max becomes positive when it should be positive but the *intermediate sums* may oscillate.
> 6. **Convergence assumption**: asserting "first term is sufficient" — for UDL on SSSS it is *almost* sufficient, but for **concentrated or partial loads** it is grossly wrong. Examiner will deduct if you claim convergence without justification.
> 7. **Units**: q in N/m², D in N·m, w in m. Mixing mm and m gives a 0.5–1 mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Navier's solution is exact** for SSSS rectangular plates under arbitrary transverse loading — no other analytical method gives a closed-form for this case.
- **The assumed form is the double sine series** because sines are the only elementary functions that vanish with zero second derivative on both ends of an interval.
- **The plate equation D ∇⁴w = q** reduces, after substitution, to a **decoupled algebraic equation for each w_mn** — making the method computationally trivial.
- **q_mn is obtained by integration** using orthogonality. For UDL, only **odd m, n contribute**; the result is $q_{mn} = 16q_0/(\pi^2 mn)$.
- **The final w_mn formula** is
  $w_{mn} = q_{mn} / \{\pi^4 D [(m/a)^2 + (n/b)^2]^2\}$.
- **For a UDL on a SSSS square plate**, the centre-deflection coefficient is the famous **0.00406 q₀a⁴/D**.
- **Bending moments** $M_x$ and $M_y$ involve both the second derivative in their own direction and the **Poisson-coupled** second derivative in the perpendicular direction; this coupling is the essential "plate effect."
- **Convergence is rapid for smooth loads** (UDL, hydrostatic) but **slow for concentrated loads** — high modes are excited significantly.
- **For ship structures**, the Navier solution is used to compute the **scantlings of bottom, side, and bulkhead plating** between stiffeners; first-mode approximation is acceptable at the **preliminary design stage**.
- **Boundary extension:** The method fails for clamped, free, or mixed edges. Use **Levy's method** (single Fourier series) for two opposite edges simply supported and the other two arbitrary, or **FEM** for arbitrary boundaries.
- **Plate constant (1 − ν²) ≈ 0.91 for steel** is a non-negligible correction that *amplifies* the stiffness of a plate over a beam of the same thickness — students should never quote D = E h³/12.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Checklist (KTU 2024):**
> - [ ] State the Kirchhoff–Love plate equation and define D
> - [ ] Write the four SSSS boundary conditions
> - [ ] State the assumed form of the double sine series
> - [ ] Derive the orthogonality-based expression for q_mn
> - [ ] Derive the decoupled equation and solve for w_mn
> - [ ] Compute q_mn for UDL
> - [ ] Apply Navier formula to compute w_max, M_x, M_y
> - [ ] Use the 0.00406 factor for a SSSS square plate under UDL
> - [ ] Identify the convergence behaviour and limitations
> - [ ] Discuss engineering applications in ship plating
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Large Deflection Analysis Buckling of Thin Plates - Equilibrium Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Large Deflection Analysis Buckling of Thin Plates - Equilibrium Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Large deflection analysis of thin plates** is a non-linear structural analysis that accounts for the geometric stiffening effect caused by the development of in-plane membrane (stretching) stresses when the lateral deflection $w$ of a buckled thin plate becomes **comparable to, or greater than, the plate thickness $t$**. In this regime, the classical linear (small deflection) theory — which assumes $w \ll t$ and neglects membrane action — is no longer valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** The **Equilibrium Method** of large deflection analysis is a *direct force-balance approach* in which the post-buckled deflected shape of the plate is assumed as a known analytical function (typically a double trigonometric series), substituted into the **non-linear von Kármán plate equations**, and the resulting **equilibrium equations are satisfied** to obtain the relationship between the applied edge load and the maximum lateral deflection $w_0$.

The **critical buckling load** predicted by small-deflection theory remains the *initiation point* of instability, but the plate does **not collapse** at $N_{cr}$. Instead, it sustains a *significantly higher* load through membrane stiffening — this is called the **post-buckling reserve strength** and is critically important in **ship plating, hatch covers, and bulkhead design**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Crumpled Sheet of Paper":**
> Take a thin A4 sheet and press its short ends together along a table edge. At a certain force, the sheet suddenly *bows out* into one or more waves — this is the **classical buckling load**. Now, push harder. The sheet *does not collapse*; it merely bows more deeply while carrying additional load. Why? Because as the middle surface **stretches** in the direction of the wave crests, it develops **membrane tension** (like a taught string) that resists further deflection. The load is now carried by **two systems** working in parallel: (1) the original plate-bending stiffness $D$, and (2) the newly activated membrane stiffness $N_x$. This is exactly what the **large deflection equilibrium method** mathematically captures.

In ship structural engineering, this explains why the **bottom plating** of a ship can survive shell buckling (e.g., between frames) without catastrophic failure — the membrane action redistributes load to the surrounding stiffened boundary.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Flexural Rigidity:** $D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ where $E$ is **Young's modulus (≈ 2.1 × 10⁵ N/mm² for mild steel)** and $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.30 for steel)**.
- **Slenderness Ratio:** $\beta = b/t$ — for ship plates, typically $40 \le \beta \le 100$.
- **Critical Stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ — note the **inverse-square dependence on $b/t$**.
- **Buckling Coefficient $k$:** For a simply supported rectangular plate under uniaxial compression, $k_{\min} = 4.0$ (for $a/b \to \infty$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Von Kármán Equations

When a plate undergoes large deflections, the strain-displacement relations must include the *non-linear* term $\frac{1}{2}\left(\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^{2}$. The resulting governing equations are the **two coupled von Kármán equations** (one for bending equilibrium, one for in-plane Airy stress function compatibility):

**Equation (1) — Bending Equilibrium:**

$$D\,\nabla^{4} w \;=\; L(w,F) \;+\; q(x,y)$$

where $L(w,F)$ is the bilinear operator (sometimes called the *Jacobian*) given by:

$$L(w,F) \;=\; \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial y^{2}} \;+\; \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x^{2}} \;-\; 2\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x\,\partial y}$$

**Equation (2) — Compatibility (in-plane Airy stress function):**

$$\nabla^{4} F \;=\; E\left[\,\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\right)^{2} \;-\; \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\,\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial observation for KTU exams:** When $w \to 0$, the right-hand side of Equation (2) vanishes, $F$ becomes the trivial solution $\frac{N_x\,y^{2}}{2}$, and Equation (1) reduces to the **classical small-deflection buckling equation** $D\nabla^{4} w = N_x\,\partial^{2}w/\partial x^{2}$. The large deflection analysis is therefore a *natural extension* that the small-deflection theory cannot capture.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Equilibrium Method

The **Equilibrium Method** proceeds as follows:

1. **Assume the deflected shape.** Use a single-term or multi-term double trigonometric series that satisfies the **geometric (essential) boundary conditions** at the plate edges.
2. **Substitute** this assumed $w(x,y)$ into the **compatibility equation** to solve for the Airy stress function $F(x,y)$.
3. **Substitute** both $w$ and $F$ into the **bending equilibrium equation**.
4. **Enforce satisfaction** of the bending equation — typically by the **Galerkin weighted-residual method** (since a single-mode series does not satisfy the PDE exactly).
5. **Solve the resulting algebraic relation** for the unknown amplitude $w_0$ (maximum deflection) as a function of applied load $N_x$.
6. **Plot / analyse** the **post-buckling load-deflection curve** $N_x$ vs. $w_0$.

### 2.3 Assumed Deflected Shape (Most Common KTU Case)

For a **simply supported rectangular plate** of dimensions $a \times b$ and thickness $t$, compressed by $N_x$ per unit length in the $x$-direction:

$$w(x,y) \;=\; w_{0}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{m\,\pi\,x}{a}\right)\,\sin\!\left(\frac{n\,\pi\,y}{b}\right)$$

where $m$ is the number of half-waves in $x$ and $n$ is the number in $y$. For the long plate ($a \gg b$) under uniaxial compression, the lowest critical load corresponds to $m = 1$ (single half-wave in $x$), and symmetry demands $n = 1$ (single half-wave across the width).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All key equations required for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic are consolidated below. **Memorise this table.**

| # | Quantity / Equation | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Flexural Rigidity | $D = \dfrac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ | $E$: N/mm², $t$: mm, $D$: N·mm |
| 2 | Linear Critical Load (per unit length) | $N_{cr} = \dfrac{k\,\pi^{2}\,D}{b^{2}}$ | $N_{cr}$: N/mm |
| 3 | Linear Critical Stress | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$ | $\sigma_{cr}$: N/mm² |
| 4 | Buckling Coefficient (SS, uniaxial) | $k = \left(\dfrac{m\,b}{a} + \dfrac{a}{m\,b}\right)^{2}$ | $k_{\min} = 4.0$ |
| 5 | Von Kármán Bending Eq. | $D\,\nabla^{4} w = L(w,F) + q$ | Operator $L$ as above |
| 6 | Von Kármán Compatibility | $\nabla^{4} F = E\!\left[\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^{\!2} - \dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right]$ | $F$: N·mm²/mm |
| 7 | Airy F.C. (uniaxial, no shear) | $F = \dfrac{N_{x}\,y^{2}}{2} + F_{0}(x,y)$ | $F_{0}$: perturbation |
| 8 | Post-buckling load ratio (single-mode) | $\dfrac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{4}\,\dfrac{w_{0}^{2}}{t^{2}}\,\dfrac{\pi^{2}}{3(1-\nu^{2})}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Effective Width (von Kármán) | $b_{e} = b\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{\max}}}$ | $b_{e}$: mm |
| 10 | Post-buckling edge stress | $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_{cr} + \dfrac{E\,\pi^{2}}{4(1-\nu^{2})}\,\dfrac{w_{0}^{2}}{b^{2}}$ | $\sigma_{\max}$: N/mm² |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **ship and offshore structural design**, large deflection analysis is *the* governing theory for:
- **Longitudinally stiffened plating** in ship hulls (where $b/t$ routinely exceeds 60).
- **Hatch covers** and **deck plating** under combined bending and hydrostatic pressure.
- **Bulkhead plating** with high aspect ratios.
- The **effective width concept** is directly used in **IS 800 (Indian Steel Code)** and **DNV-RP-C201** for plate girder and stiffened panel design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Buckling Load (Baseline)

This derivation establishes the *small-deflection* benchmark against which large-deflection behaviour is compared.

**Starting point:** For a simply supported plate, assume a deflected shape with $m$ half-waves in $x$ and $n$ half-waves in $y$:

$$w(x,y) \;=\; A_{mn}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{m\,\pi\,x}{a}\right)\,\sin\!\left(\frac{n\,\pi\,y}{b}\right)$$

**Step 1 —** Apply the biharmonic operator $\nabla^{4} = \left(\dfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2}}{\partial y^{2}}\right)^{2}$. For a sinusoidal mode:

$$\nabla^{4}\!\left[\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)\right] \;=\; \pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

**Step 2 —** Substitute into the *linear* plate buckling equation $D\,\nabla^{4} w = N_x\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}$:

$$D\,\pi^{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2} A_{mn} \;=\; -N_x\,\frac{m^{2}\pi^{2}}{a^{2}}\,A_{mn}$$

**Step 3 —** Cancel $A_{mn}$ (non-trivial solution) and take the magnitude:

$$N_{x} \;=\; D\,\pi^{2}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{\!2}\,\frac{a^{2}}{m^{2}}$$

**Step 4 —** Factor to introduce the aspect ratio $\alpha = a/b$:

$$N_{x} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,D}{b^{2}}\,\frac{\left(m\,\alpha\right)^{2} + n^{2}}{m\,\alpha}\,\frac{1}{m\,\alpha}\,(\text{wait, re-factor properly})$$

$$N_{x} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}\,D}{b^{2}}\,\left(\frac{m\,b}{a} + \frac{a}{m\,b}\right)^{\!2} \quad \text{for } n = 1$$

**Step 5 —** Define the **buckling coefficient** $k$:

$$\boxed{\,N_{cr} \;=\; \frac{k\,\pi^{2}\,D}{b^{2}}, \quad \text{where } k = \left(\frac{m\,b}{a} + \frac{a}{m\,b}\right)^{\!2}\,}$$

**Step 6 —** Convert to stress: $N_{cr} = \sigma_{cr}\,t$, and substitute $D$:

$$\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \frac{k\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{\!2}$$

For the long plate limit ($\alpha \to \infty$), the optimal $m$ is the integer nearest to $a/b$, and $k \to k_{\min} = 4.0$.

### 3.2 Full Derivation of the Post-Buckling Load–Deflection Relation (Equilibrium Method)

**Problem:** A simply supported rectangular plate ($a \times b \times t$) is loaded by a uniform in-plane compressive force $N_x$ per unit length on its $x$-edges. Determine the relationship between $N_x$ and the maximum lateral deflection $w_0$ when $w_0$ is **comparable to or larger than $t$**.

#### Step A — Assume the Deflected Shape

For the lowest post-buckling mode (long plate, single half-wave in $x$, single half-wave in $y$):

$$w(x,y) \;=\; w_{0}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi\,x}{a}\right)\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi\,y}{b}\right)$$

**Justification:** This satisfies $w = 0$ and $M = 0$ at all four simply supported edges. The peak deflection is $w_0$, attained at the plate centre $(a/2,\;b/2)$.

#### Step B — Compute the Right-Hand Side of the Compatibility Equation

We need:

$$E\left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\right)^{\!2} - \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right]$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = w_{0}\,\frac{\pi}{a}\,\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = w_{0}\,\frac{\pi}{b}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y} = w_{0}\,\frac{\pi^{2}}{a\,b}\,\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = -w_{0}\,\frac{\pi^{2}}{a^{2}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} = -w_{0}\,\frac{\pi^{2}}{b^{2}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Now form the expression:

$$\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\right)^{\!2} - \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}} = w_{0}^{2}\,\frac{\pi^{4}}{a^{2}\,b^{2}}\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right) - w_{0}^{2}\,\frac{\pi^{4}}{a^{2}\,b^{2}}\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Factor $w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{4}/(a^{2}b^{2})$:

$$= \frac{w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{4}}{a^{2}\,b^{2}}\left[\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right) - \sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)\right]$$

Apply the **double-angle identity** $\cos^{2}\theta\cos^{2}\phi - \sin^{2}\theta\sin^{2}\phi = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[\cos 2\theta + \cos 2\phi\bigr]/2$ — simplified to:

$$= \frac{w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{4}}{2\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}\left[\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{a}\right) + \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right)\right]$$

#### Step C — Solve the Compatibility Equation for $F$

The full Airy stress function is written as:

$$F(x,y) \;=\; \frac{N_{x}\,y^{2}}{2} \;+\; F_{0}(x,y)$$

The first term accounts for the pre-buckling uniform compression, and $F_0$ captures the perturbation induced by bending. Substituting into $\nabla^{4} F = E\,[\text{RHS}]$:

$$\nabla^{4} F_{0} \;=\; \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{4}}{2\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}\left[\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{a}\right) + \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right)\right]$$

The general solution (satisfying SS boundary conditions) is:

$$F_{0}(x,y) \;=\; \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}}{32}\left[\frac{\cos(2\pi x/a)}{1/a^{2} + 1/b^{2}}\cdot\frac{2}{a^{2}}\cdot\frac{1}{(\text{...})} \right]$$

Substituting back, the biharmonic of a cosine of the form $A\cos(2\pi x/a)$ is $A\,(2\pi/a)^{4}\cos(2\pi x/a)$. Equating coefficients:

$$A_{1}\!\left(\frac{2\pi}{a}\right)^{\!4} = \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{4}}{2\,a^{2}\,b^{2}} \;\Rightarrow\; A_{1} = \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,a^{2}}{32\,b^{2}}$$

Similarly, $A_{2} = \dfrac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,b^{2}}{32\,a^{2}}$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,F(x,y) = \frac{N_{x}\,y^{2}}{2} + \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}}{32}\!\left[\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi x}{a}\right) + \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right)\right]\,}$$

#### Step D — Substitute into the Bending Equation

Compute $\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\dfrac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial y^{2}}$ and the analogous term:

$$\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial y^{2}} = N_{x} - \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{2}}{8\,a^{2}}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right)$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial y^{2}} = -w_{0}\,\frac{\pi^{2}}{a^{2}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi y}{b}\right)\!\left[N_{x} - \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{2}}{8\,a^{2}}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right)\right]$$

By analogous derivation, the cross term $\dfrac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\dfrac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x^{2}}$ produces the symmetric expression.

#### Step E — Apply Galerkin's Procedure (Enforce Equilibrium)

Multiply the residual of the bending equation by the assumed mode $w(x,y)$ and integrate over the plate area $\bigl[0,a\bigr] \times \bigl[0,b\bigr]$. The orthogonality of the sine functions eliminates all but the relevant terms.

After performing the integrations (using $\int_0^a \sin^{2}(\pi x/a)\,dx = a/2$ and $\int_0^a \cos^{2}(2\pi x/a)\,dx = a/2$):

$$D\,\frac{\pi^{4}}{b^{4}}(1 + \alpha^{2})^{2}\,w_{0} \;=\; N_{x}\,\frac{\pi^{2}}{a^{2}}\,w_{0} \;-\; \frac{\pi^{4}\,E\,w_{0}^{3}}{8\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}\!\left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\alpha^{2}}{2}\right)$$

Recognising that the *linear* part gives $N_{x} = N_{cr}$ when $w_0 = 0$, we subtract to obtain the **post-buckling correction**:

$$\boxed{\,\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} \;=\; 1 \;+\; \frac{w_{0}^{2}}{t^{2}}\,\frac{\pi^{2}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,\frac{1}{4} \,=\, 1 + \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{w_{0}^{2}}{t^{2}}\cdot\frac{\pi^{2}}{3(1-\nu^{2})}\,}$$

This is the **key result** of the equilibrium method for a simply supported long plate under uniaxial compression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** The factor $w_0^2 / t^2$ in the equation makes the post-buckling behaviour strongly **non-linear**. As $w_0 \to 0$, $N_x \to N_{cr}$ — recovering the linear result. As $w_0$ grows, the plate carries **more load** than $N_{cr}$. The plate never collapses at $N_{cr}$; rather, it has a *graceful, ductile post-buckling reserve*.

### 3.3 Effective Width Derivation (Von Kármán)

The **effective width** is the engineering device used to convert the complex 2D post-buckled stress distribution into an equivalent uniformly-stressed strip of width $b_e < b$.

**Definition (energy-equivalent / load-equivalent):**

$$b_{e} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total compressive force carried by plate}}{\text{Edge stress at the supported boundary } \sigma_{\max}}$$

$$b_{e} \;=\; \frac{N_{x}\,b}{\sigma_{\max}}$$

Substituting the post-buckling relations $N_{x} = N_{cr}\!\left[1 + c\,(w_0/t)^{2}\right]$ and $\sigma_{\max} = \sigma_{cr}\!\left[1 + c\,(w_0/t)^{2}\right]$ (same correction factor), the ratio simplifies beautifully:

$$b_{e} \;=\; b\,\frac{\sigma_{cr}\!\left[1 + c\,(w_0/t)^{2}\right]}{\sigma_{\max}} \;\xrightarrow{\sigma_{\max} \to \sigma_{y}}\; b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{y}}}$$

**Final form:**

$$\boxed{\,b_{e} \;=\; b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{y}}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $\sigma_{\max}$ is taken to the yield stress $\sigma_y$, the effective width is fixed by *geometry alone* and is the basis of design rules in **IS 800, DNV, and Lloyd's Register** for stiffened plating.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams below follow the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** convention, since the topic involves continuum mechanics where direct physical drawings (buckle mode shapes) are best represented as *mathematical block interactions* within the equilibrium method's solution algorithm.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Solution Algorithm of the Equilibrium Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Plate Geometry a, b, t, Material E, nu] --> B[Compute Flexural Rigidity D]
    B --> C[Compute Linear Critical Load Ncr from k min pi squared D by b squared]
    C --> D[Assume Deflected Shape w x y as single mode double sine series with amplitude w0]
    D --> E[Substitute w into Compatibility Eq to get Airy F x y]
    E --> F[Compute partial derivatives of w and F: w_xx, w_yy, w_xy, F_xx, F_yy]
    F --> G[Form Bilinear Operator L of w comma F]
    G --> H[Substitute into Bending Equilibrium D nabla4 w equals L of w comma F]
    H --> I[Apply Galerkin Weighted Residual: multiply by w and integrate over plate area]
    I --> J[Use Orthogonality of sine functions to collapse double integral to single term]
    J --> K[Solve Algebraic Equation for Nx as function of w0]
    K --> L[Derive Post-Buckling Relation Nx by Ncr equals 1 plus correction in w0 by t]
    L --> M[Derive Effective Width b effective equals b times square root of sigma cr by sigma max]
    M --> N[END: Report Nx versus w0 curve, b_e, and reserve strength]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Physical Interpretation: Bending vs. Membrane Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Applied Edge Compression Nx] --> Q{Deflection Regime}
    Q -->|w less than t| R[Small Deflection: Only Bending Stiffness D active]
    Q -->|w comparable to t| S[Large Deflection: Bending D plus Membrane Nx action]
    R --> R1[Linear Theory: Buckling equals Collapse]
    S --> S1[Non-Linear Theory: Post-Buckling Reserve Activated]
    S1 --> S2[Membrane Tension forms at Wave Crests]
    S2 --> S3[Stretched Middle Surface carries additional Nx beyond Ncr]
    S3 --> S4[Effective Width b_e less than b becomes Design Parameter]
    S4 --> S5[Engineering Design: IS 800 / DNV Rules for Ship Plating]
    R1 --> T1[Result: Nx max equals Ncr]
    S5 --> T2[Result: Nx max greater than Ncr by Membrane Reserve]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Coupling Between the Two Von Kármán Equations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Assumed Deflection w x y] --> B1[Compute nabla4 F from compatibility]
    B1 --> C1[Airy Stress Function F x y]
    C1 --> D1[Substitute into bending eq]
    A1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Galerkin enforces equilibrium]
    E1 --> F1[Algebraic equation for Nx versus w0]
    F1 -->|Iterate w0| A1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guide:** The double-arrow between the two PDEs (A1 → C1 and C1 ↔ A1) emphasises that $w$ and $F$ are *coupled* — the Airy stress function depends non-linearly on the deflection, and the bending equation depends linearly on $F$ but bi-linearly on $w$. The Galerkin procedure reduces this infinite-dimensional coupling to a **single scalar algebraic equation** in $w_0$, which is the *entire* essence of the equilibrium method.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Similar Model)]**
*State the Von Kármán equations governing the large deflection behaviour of a thin plate and explain the physical meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Von Kármán equations for a thin plate undergoing large deflections are a pair of coupled non-linear partial differential equations:

**Bending equilibrium:**

$$D\,\nabla^{4} w \;=\; \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial y^{2}} \;+\; \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x^{2}} \;-\; 2\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\,\frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial x\,\partial y} \;+\; q$$

**Compatibility (in-plane Airy stress function):**

$$\nabla^{4} F \;=\; E\left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\right)^{\!2} - \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right]$$

- $D\,\nabla^{4}w$ represents the **restoring bending moment per unit area** of the plate, with $D = Et^{3}/[12(1-\nu^{2})]$.
- The bilinear term in $F$ and $w$ represents the **transverse action of the in-plane membrane forces** (stretching of the middle surface) on the plate — this is the **non-linear coupling** that distinguishes large-deflection theory from linear theory.
- $q$ is the **lateral (transverse) load** per unit area.
- The compatibility equation **relates the Airy stress function $F$** to the square of slope gradients of $w$, capturing the *stretching of the middle surface* — the source of membrane stiffening.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks only if all four terms are explicitly written and at least one physical interpretation is given.)*

---

**Q.A2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar Model)]**
*What is meant by "post-buckling reserve strength" of a thin plate? Why is it important in ship structural design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Post-buckling reserve strength** is the additional load-carrying capacity of a thin plate **beyond the classical (linear) critical buckling load $N_{cr}$**. The classical theory predicts plate failure at $N_{cr}$, but the large-deflection analysis shows that the plate continues to carry increasing load through the development of **transverse membrane (stretching) stresses** in the buckled wave pattern.

It is **critically important in ship structural design** because:

1. **Ship plating is invariably thin** ($b/t$ between 40 and 100), and **linear buckling** is therefore inevitable under service loads. The *true collapse* load is significantly higher than $N_{cr}$ due to membrane reserve.
2. Design codes (IS 800, DNV-RP-C201, Lloyd's Register) use the **effective width concept** derived from large-deflection analysis to compute the *ultimate* load capacity of stiffened panels.
3. Ignoring post-buckling reserve leads to **grossly over-conservative and uneconomical** designs, particularly for bottom shell plating, hatch covers, and bulkheads.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for engineering importance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse the equilibrium method with the energy method.** The equilibrium method uses **Galerkin's procedure** to enforce the *von Kármán PDE* directly; the energy method minimises the *total potential energy functional* (covered in a separate topic). Mark loss of 2–3 marks is common.
> - Always explicitly state the **assumed deflected shape** (typically $w = w_0 \sin(\pi x/a)\sin(\pi y/b)$) at the *start* of every derivation. Students who omit this lose 1 mark.
> - The post-buckling correction term is **dimensionally** $[w_0^{2}/t^{2}]$, which is dimensionless — verify this before writing the final formula. Examiners check dimensional consistency.
> - Do NOT use the **small-deflection formula** $\sigma_{cr} = k\pi^{2}E(t/b)^{2}/[12(1-\nu^{2})]$ when the question asks for **post-buckling behaviour**. The small-deflection result gives the *initiation* of buckling, not the *reserve strength*.

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

A simply supported rectangular mild-steel plate has dimensions $a = 1200$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $t = 10$ mm. It is subjected to uniform uniaxial compression in the $x$-direction. Using the **small-deflection theory**, compute the **linear critical buckling load per unit length** and the **critical buckling stress**. Given: $E = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.30$.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the aspect ratio:**

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{1200}{600} = 2.0$$

**Step 2 — For SS plate under uniaxial compression, the buckling coefficient is:**

$$k = \left(\frac{m\,b}{a} + \frac{a}{m\,b}\right)^{\!2} = \left(\frac{m}{\alpha} + \frac{\alpha}{m}\right)^{\!2}$$

The integer $m$ that minimises $k$ is $m = 1$ when $\alpha = 2$. Verify:

$$k = \left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{1}\right)^{\!2} = (2.5)^{2} = 6.25$$

**Step 3 — Compute flexural rigidity:**

$$D = \frac{E\,t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})} = \frac{(2.1 \times 10^{5})(10)^{3}}{12(1 - 0.09)} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{5} \times 1000}{12 \times 0.91} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{8}}{10.92} \approx 1.923 \times 10^{7} \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute critical buckling load per unit length:**

$$N_{cr} = \frac{k\,\pi^{2}\,D}{b^{2}} = \frac{6.25 \times \pi^{2} \times 1.923 \times 10^{7}}{(600)^{2}}$$

$$N_{cr} = \frac{6.25 \times 9.8696 \times 1.923 \times 10^{7}}{3.6 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{1.186 \times 10^{9}}{3.6 \times 10^{5}} \approx 3295 \text{ N/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute critical buckling stress:**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{cr}}{t} = \frac{3295}{10} = 329.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 330 \text{ MPa}$$

*Cross-check using the direct formula:*

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k\,\pi^{2}\,E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2} = \frac{6.25 \times 9.8696 \times 2.1 \times 10^{5}}{10.92} \times \left(\frac{10}{600}\right)^{2}$$

$$= 118630 \times \frac{1}{3600} \approx 329.5 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark$$

**Answer:** $N_{cr} \approx 3295$ N/mm; $\sigma_{cr} \approx 330$ MPa.

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks]**

For the same plate, the maximum lateral deflection under service loading is observed to be $w_0 = 15$ mm. Using the **Von Kármán large-deflection equilibrium method**, estimate the **post-buckling load ratio** $N_x / N_{cr}$ and the **effective width** assuming the edge stress reaches yield $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the equilibrium-method post-buckling relation (single-mode):**

$$\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + \frac{1}{4}\cdot\frac{w_{0}^{2}}{t^{2}}\cdot\frac{\pi^{2}}{3(1-\nu^{2})}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the correction term:**

$$\frac{w_{0}^{2}}{t^{2}} = \frac{(15)^{2}}{(10)^{2}} = 2.25$$

$$\frac{\pi^{2}}{3(1-\nu^{2})} = \frac{9.8696}{3(0.91)} = \frac{9.8696}{2.73} \approx 3.615$$

$$\text{Correction} = \frac{1}{4} \times 2.25 \times 3.615 = 2.034$$

**Step 3 — Compute the load ratio:**

$$\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + 2.034 = 3.034$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actual post-buckling load:**

$$N_{x} = 3.034 \times 3295 \approx 9997 \text{ N/mm} \approx 10.0 \text{ kN/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the effective width:**

$$b_{e} = b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{y}}} = 600 \times \sqrt{\frac{329.5}{250}} = 600 \times \sqrt{1.318} = 600 \times 1.148 \approx 689 \text{ mm}$$

*Note: $b_e > b$ here because $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$, which physically means the plate can be loaded to the *yield* stress before buckling becomes a concern, and the *full width* is mobilised. In this regime, the effective-width formula should be capped at $b_e = b$. The correct design interpretation is: the plate does not buckle before yielding — the limit state is yielding, not buckling.*

**Engineering conclusion:** Since $\sigma_{cr} = 330$ MPa $> \sigma_y = 250$ MPa, the plate is **stocky enough** that *yielding governs* rather than buckling. The post-buckling reserve is therefore not the *governing* design criterion here, but the calculation demonstrates the **method**.

**Answer:** $N_x / N_{cr} \approx 3.03$; $b_e \approx 689$ mm (effectively capped at $b = 600$ mm since $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$).

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**Q.B(a) [7 marks] — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**

Derive the **governing Von Kármán equations** for large deflection bending of a thin elastic plate. Clearly state the assumptions and explain the role of the Airy stress function.

**Model Solution Outline (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Plate thickness $t$ is much smaller than the in-plane dimensions.
2. Deflection $w$ is finite (comparable to $t$) but rotations $\partial w / \partial x$, $\partial w / \partial y$ are still small (Kirchhoff hypothesis retained).
3. The in-plane stress resultants are derived from an Airy stress function $F(x,y)$ such that $N_x = \partial^{2}F/\partial y^{2}$, $N_y = \partial^{2}F/\partial x^{2}$, $N_{xy} = -\partial^{2}F/\partial x\,\partial y$.
4. The material is elastic, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law in the plane.

**Derivation of Bending Equation (3 marks):**

The non-linear strain-displacement relations include the von Kármán strain $\epsilon^{0}_{x} = \partial u/\partial x + \frac{1}{2}(\partial w/\partial x)^{2}$ (and similar for $y$). After eliminating in-plane displacements via the Airy stress function and applying the Kirchhoff plate equation, we obtain:

$$D\,\nabla^{4} w \;=\; L(w,F) \;+\; q$$

with the bilinear operator $L$ as defined in Section 2.1.

**Compatibility Equation (2 marks):**

Eliminating the Airy function via the in-plane equilibrium equations and the strain compatibility condition $\partial^{2}\epsilon^{0}_{x}/\partial y^{2} + \partial^{2}\epsilon^{0}_{y}/\partial x^{2} = \partial^{2}\gamma^{0}_{xy}/\partial x\,\partial y$ yields:

$$\nabla^{4} F \;=\; E\left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x\,\partial y}\right)^{\!2} - \frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial y^{2}}\right]$$

**Role of the Airy stress function (1 mark):** $F$ automatically satisfies the in-plane equilibrium equations $\partial N_x/\partial x + \partial N_{xy}/\partial y = 0$ identically, and reduces the in-plane problem to a *single scalar PDE* coupled to the bending equation.

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks]**

A simply supported long plate ($a/b \gg 1$, simply supported on all four edges) is loaded uniaxially in $x$ by $N_x$. Using the **single-mode assumption** $w = w_0\sin(\pi x/a)\sin(\pi y/b)$ and **Galerkin's method**, derive the post-buckling load-deflection relation:

$$\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + \frac{\pi^{2}\,w_{0}^{2}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})\,t^{2}}$$

Show all intermediate steps. *(Note: The KTU textbook statement of the formula may include additional terms depending on the assumed mode; the 1/4 factor in some textbook versions corresponds to a different choice of $\nu$.)*

**Step-by-step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark) —** Substitute $w$ into the compatibility RHS:

$$E\left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x \partial y}\right)^{2} - \frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right] = \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{4}}{2\,a^{2}\,b^{2}}\left[\cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} + \cos\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right]$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) —** Integrate $\nabla^{4} F_0$ to obtain:

$$F(x,y) = \frac{N_{x}\,y^{2}}{2} + \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}}{32}\left[\frac{a^{2}}{b^{2}}\cos\frac{2\pi x}{a} + \frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\cos\frac{2\pi y}{b}\right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) —** Compute the perturbation stress $\partial^{2}F_0/\partial y^{2}$ at the loaded edge:

$$\left.\frac{\partial^{2}F}{\partial y^{2}}\right|_{y=0,b} = N_{x} \pm \frac{E\,w_{0}^{2}\,\pi^{2}}{8\,a^{2}}\cos\frac{2\pi x}{a}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) —** Form the bending residual and integrate over the plate area using orthogonality:

$$\int_0^{a}\!\int_0^{b}\!\left[D\,\nabla^{4}w - L(w,F)\right]\sin\frac{\pi x}{a}\sin\frac{\pi y}{b}\,dx\,dy = 0$$

After integration, the cross-terms involving $N_x \cdot w_0$ and $N_{cr} \cdot w_0$ emerge from the linear part, and the $w_0^{3}$ correction emerges from the non-linear term:

$$N_{x} = N_{cr} + \frac{\pi^{2}\,E\,w_{0}^{2}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})\,t^{2}}\,N_{cr}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) —** Divide by $N_{cr}$ to obtain the dimensionless post-buckling relation:

$$\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + \frac{\pi^{2}\,E}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})\,E\,\left(\frac{k\,\pi^{2}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\right)\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}}\cdot\frac{w_{0}^{2}}{t^{2}}$$

Simplifying using $\sigma_{cr} = N_{cr}/t$ and $k=4$:

$$\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + \frac{\pi^{2}\,w_{0}^{2}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})\,t^{2}}$$

**Step 6 (2 marks) —** Discussion:

- For $w_0 = 0$: $N_x = N_{cr}$ (recovers linear theory).
- For $w_0 = t$: $N_x / N_{cr} = 1 + \pi^{2}/[12(1-\nu^{2})] \approx 2.89$ (for $\nu = 0.30$).
- For $w_0 = 2t$: $N_x / N_{cr} \approx 6.6$ — the plate carries more than six times the classical critical load.
- The plate has a **strong post-buckling reserve**, justifying the design philosophy of permitting shell buckling in ship plating.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Large deflection theory is non-linear** because of the term $\frac{1}{2}(\partial w/\partial x)^{2}$ in the strain-displacement relation.
- **Von Kármán equations = two coupled PDEs** (bending + compatibility), and they reduce to linear buckling theory when $w \to 0$.
- **Equilibrium method** = assume deflected shape → substitute → solve via **Galerkin's procedure**. **Energy method** = minimise total potential energy.
- **Critical buckling stress** (linear): $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}(t/b)^{2}$ — depends on the *buckling coefficient $k$* and the *slenderness ratio* $b/t$.
- **Buckling coefficient $k$** for SS uniaxial plate: $k = (m\,b/a + a/(m\,b))^{2}$; $k_{\min} = 4.0$ for $a/b \to \infty$.
- **Single-mode assumption** for lowest mode: $w = w_0\sin(\pi x/a)\sin(\pi y/b)$.
- **Post-buckling load ratio** (single-mode equilibrium method):

$$\frac{N_{x}}{N_{cr}} = 1 + \frac{\pi^{2}\,w_{0}^{2}}{12\,(1-\nu^{2})\,t^{2}}$$

- **Effective width** (von Kármán): $b_e = b\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_{\max}}$; capped at $b$ when $\sigma_{cr} \ge \sigma_y$.
- **Post-buckling reserve strength** is *always* present in thin ship plates; classical buckling is *not* collapse.
- **Common student mistakes to avoid:** confusing the equilibrium and energy methods; using the linear formula in the non-linear regime; forgetting dimensional analysis on the correction term; omitting the assumed mode in the derivation.
- **Numerical sanity check (for $\nu = 0.30$):** $\pi^{2}/[12(1-\nu^{2})] \approx 2.89$. So when $w_0 = t$, the load ratio $\approx 3.9$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Stiffened Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Stiffened Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **stiffened plate** is a structural element consisting of a flat plate reinforced by a system of integral stiffeners (girders, beams, or ribs) attached to one or both sides. In ship structural engineering, stiffened plates form the basic load-bearing unit of hull shells, decks, bulkheads, and superstructures. The stiffeners run parallel to each other, dividing the plate into a series of rectangular panels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A stiffened plate is an assemblage of a plating and a series of equally spaced parallel stiffeners, designed to act monolithically in resisting in-plane (membrane) and out-of-plane (bending) loads. The analysis determines the effective distribution of load between the plate and the stiffener, leading to the concept of *effective breadth*.

### 1.2 Classification of Stiffened Plates

| **Type** | **Description** | **Typical Ship Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniaxially Stiffened** | Stiffeners in one direction only | Side shell, weather deck, bottom plating |
| **Orthogonally Stiffened (Grillage)** | Stiffeners in two perpendicular directions | Bottom shell, strength deck, large panels |
| **Diagonally Stiffened** | Stiffeners placed at angles | Special high-stress regions, bilge areas |
| **Stiffened Plate with Longitudinals** | Stiffeners parallel to direction of principal stress | Hull girder longitudinal members |

> [!NOTE]
> In modern shipbuilding (as per **IACS Common Structural Rules** and **DNV standards**), longitudinal stiffening dominates because longitudinal stresses from hull girder bending are far higher than transverse bending stresses.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bookshelf Analogy"

Imagine holding a **large thin book (representing the plate)** and pressing down on its edge. The book bends and buckles easily because it is very thin. Now, glue a **thin wooden strip (representing the stiffener)** along the length of the book. When you press down again, the strip and the adjacent portion of the book act **together** to resist the load. 

The key insight is that **not the entire width of the book** participates in resisting the load — only a certain width near the strip, called the **effective breadth**, carries the load. The rest of the plate merely "follows along" with little stress.

In a ship:
- **Plate** = hull plating (thin, wide, prone to buckling)
- **Stiffener** = longitudinal or transverse web
- **Load** = hydrostatic pressure, hull girder bending
- **Effective breadth** = the portion of the plate that effectively collaborates with the stiffener

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard values are typically used in ship structural analysis:
> - **Poisson's ratio for steel:** $\nu = 0.3$
> - **Young's modulus for shipbuilding steel:** $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$
> - **Yield stress (mild steel):** $\sigma_y = 235 \text{ MPa}$
> - **Yield stress (high-tensile steel):** $\sigma_y = 315 \text{ MPa}$ or $355 \text{ MPa}$
> - **Standard unit thickness:** $t$ in **mm**, span $L$ in **m**

### 1.5 Why Analyse Stiffened Plates?

The principal reasons are:

1. **To find the actual stress distribution** in the plating and the stiffener.
2. **To determine the effective breadth** so that the stiffened plate can be reduced to an equivalent beam for design.
3. **To check buckling strength** (local panel buckling between stiffeners, and overall stiffened panel buckling).
4. **To ensure adequate ultimate strength** under extreme loads.
5. **To comply with classification society rules** (IACS CSR, DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Loading on Stiffened Plates

A stiffened plate in a ship can be subjected to the following load combinations:

- **In-plane compression** from hull girder bending
- **In-plane shear** from transverse loads or torsional effects
- **Lateral pressure** (hydrostatic, sea pressure, cargo pressure)
- **Combined in-plane + lateral loads**

The **stiffened plate analysis** problem essentially asks: *"Given a load, how much of the plate width acts with the stiffener in resisting that load?"*

## 2.2 The Effective Breadth (Effective Width) Concept

### 2.2.1 The Physical Problem

Consider a long rectangular plate of breadth $b$ and length $L$, supported along the two long edges by stiffeners. A uniformly distributed load $q$ is applied transversely (perpendicular to the stiffeners). The plate deflects, and the stress distribution across the breadth is **non-uniform**:

- Maximum at the stiffener (plate-stiffener intersection)
- Minimum at the middle of the panel (midway between stiffeners)

The deflection of the plate is non-uniform: it deflects more at the middle and less at the supports (where the stiffeners hold it).

### 2.2.2 Definition of Effective Breadth

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Breadth ($b_e$):** The width of plate which, when assumed to carry a uniform stress equal to the maximum stress at the plate-stiffener junction, will carry the same total load as the actual plate with its non-uniform stress distribution.

Mathematically:

$$\int_0^b \sigma_x(x) \, dx = b_e \cdot \sigma_{\max}$$

The **efficiency** of the plate is given by the ratio $\frac{b_e}{b}$, which is always less than or equal to **1**.

### 2.2.3 Classical Timoshenko Solution

For a **simply supported long plate** under uniform lateral load, Timoshenko derived the effective breadth. The plate equation is:

$$D \nabla^4 w = q$$

where $D = \frac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the plate.

The deflection is obtained as a double Fourier series, and integrating the reaction along the supports gives the load carried by the stiffener. The result is the classical formula:

$$\frac{b_e}{b} = \frac{1}{\beta} \left[ \tanh(\beta) + \frac{2}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^3} \tanh(n\beta) \right]$$

where:
- $\beta = \dfrac{\pi b}{2 L}$ is the **dimensionless breadth parameter**
- $L$ is the span between transverse supports
- $b$ is the spacing between longitudinal stiffeners

### 2.2.4 Simplified Approximations

For design office use, the following approximations are used:

**For long plates ($\beta \to \infty$):**

$$\frac{b_e}{b} \approx \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{2L}{\pi b}$$

A commonly used approximation is:

$$b_e \approx 0.19 \, L$$

> [!NOTE]
> The rule $b_e \approx 0.19L$ is widely used in ship design for **continuous stiffened plating** between primary supports. This is the value recommended in many classification society rules.

**For short plates ($\beta \to 0$):**

$$b_e \approx b$$

This means the entire plate width is effective when the span is small relative to the spacing.

**For intermediate cases (Faulkner's approximation):**

$$\frac{b_e}{b} = \frac{2}{\beta} \left( \frac{1}{\pi} + \frac{\beta - 1}{8} \right) \text{ for } 1 \leq \beta \leq 3$$

### 2.3 Effective Breadth under In-Plane Loading (Hull Girder Bending)

When the stiffened plate is loaded by in-plane compression from hull girder bending, the effective breadth concept is also applied, but the analysis is more complex. The plate is **simply supported** on the stiffeners and subjected to in-plane compressive strain $\varepsilon$.

The effective breadth in this case is often given by:

$$\frac{b_e}{b} = \frac{1 - \nu^2}{\beta} \left[ \frac{\tanh(\beta)}{\beta} \right]$$

For long plates: $b_e \approx \dfrac{2L}{\pi b}(1-\nu^2) \cdot b = \dfrac{2L(1-\nu^2)}{\pi}$

With $\nu = 0.3$: $b_e \approx 0.182 \, L$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is very close to the $0.19L$ rule, confirming the consistency of the approximation.

## 2.4 The Equivalent Beam Concept

Once the effective breadth $b_e$ is determined, the stiffened plate is reduced to a **tee-section** or **angle-section** beam:

- **Web** = the stiffener
- **Flange** = the plating of width $b_e$ on each side (or $b_e/2$ on each side of the web)

The **section modulus** of this equivalent beam is:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area of the combined plating + stiffener, and $y$ is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme fibre.

The bending stress is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $M$ is the bending moment in the equivalent beam.

## 2.5 Orthotropic Plate Theory

A uniaxially stiffened plate is mechanically equivalent to an **orthotropic plate** with different bending stiffnesses in the two principal directions:

- **Longitudinal (stiffener) direction:** High flexural rigidity
- **Transverse direction:** Low flexural rigidity (just the plate)

The equivalent orthotropic plate has:

$$D_x = \frac{E I_s}{s}$$

$$D_y = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

where $I_s$ is the second moment of area of the stiffener (including effective plating) and $s$ is the stiffener spacing.

The Poisson's ratios for the equivalent plate are:

$$\nu_x = \nu, \quad \nu_y = \nu \frac{D_y}{D_x}$$

and

$$D_{xy} = \frac{G t^3}{12} + \frac{G I_T}{2s}$$

where $I_T$ is the St. Venant torsion constant of the stiffener.

## 2.6 Buckling of Stiffened Plates

Two distinct buckling modes are analysed:

1. **Plate panel buckling** (local): The plating between stiffeners buckles like a simply supported plate under in-plane compression.
   $$\sigma_{cr,p} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$
   with $k = 4.0$ for simply supported long plate under uniform compression.

2. **Overall stiffened panel buckling** (Euler-type): The stiffener together with the effective plating buckles as a column on an elastic foundation (provided by the surrounding plate).
   $$\sigma_{cr,o} = \frac{C \pi^2 E I_e}{A_e L^2}$$

   where $C$ is a boundary condition factor (1.0 for pinned-pinned, 0.25 for fixed-fixed) and $A_e$ is the effective cross-sectional area.

The actual buckling stress is the **smaller** of the two:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \min(\sigma_{cr,p}, \sigma_{cr,o})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In ship design, **overlapping** of these two modes gives the **inter-column buckling** behaviour, and the actual critical stress is determined by elastic-plastic analysis (as per IACS CSR).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| Plate flexural rigidity | $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | Bending of plate panels |
| Breadth parameter | $\beta = \dfrac{\pi b}{2L}$ | Effective breadth calculation |
| Effective breadth (long plate, lateral) | $b_e \approx 0.19 L$ | Ship design office approximation |
| Effective breadth (long plate, in-plane) | $b_e \approx 0.182 L$ | Hull girder bending analysis |
| Effective breadth ratio (general) | $\dfrac{b_e}{b} = \dfrac{\tanh \beta}{\beta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\beta} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{2}{\pi}\sum \cdots \right)$ | Rigorous analysis |
| Section modulus of stiffened plate | $Z = \dfrac{I_{eff}}{y_{extreme}}$ | Bending stress calculation |
| Bending stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Direct stress check |
| Plate panel buckling stress | $\sigma_{cr,p} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | Local buckling |
| Overall buckling stress | $\sigma_{cr,o} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_e}{A_e L^2}$ | Euler column buckling |
| Shear buckling coefficient | $k_\tau = 5.35 + \dfrac{4}{\alpha^2}$ for $\alpha \geq 1$ | Shear buckling of plate |
| Effective plating thickness (for section modulus) | $t_{eq} = t$ (full) or reduced | IACS CSR allows reduced |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analysis of stiffened plates is essential in:

- **Hull girder design** — for calculating the section modulus of the entire midship section.
- **Plate panel design** — to determine plate thickness that won't buckle.
- **Stiffener sizing** — to determine web height, thickness, and flange dimensions.
- **Ultimate strength assessment** — for progressive collapse and crashworthiness analysis.
- **Fatigue analysis** — to compute hot-spot stresses at stiffener-plate intersections.
- **Vibration analysis** — to avoid resonance with propeller and machinery excitations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Breadth for a Simply Supported Plate Under Uniform Lateral Load

### Step 1: Problem Setup

Consider a rectangular plate with:
- Length $L$ in the $x$-direction (between transverse supports)
- Breadth $b$ in the $y$-direction (between longitudinal stiffeners)
- Uniform lateral pressure $q$ acting in the $z$-direction
- All four edges **simply supported**

The plate equation is:

$$D \left( \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2 \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4} \right) = q$$

where $D$ is the flexural rigidity of the plate per unit width.

### Step 2: Series Solution (Navier's Method)

The deflection is expressed as a double Fourier series:

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_{mn} \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{L}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

For a uniformly distributed load $q$, the load is also expanded as a double series:

$$q = \sum_{m=1,3,5,...} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...} \frac{16 q}{\pi^2 m n} \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{L}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Solve

Substituting into the plate equation:

$$a_{mn} = \frac{16 q}{\pi^4 m n D \left[ \left(\frac{m}{L}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 \right]^2}$$

So the deflection is:

$$w(x,y) = \frac{16 q}{\pi^4 D} \sum_{m=1,3,5,...} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...} \frac{\sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{L}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)}{m n \left[ \left(\frac{m}{L}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{n}{b}\right)^2 \right]^2}$$

### Step 4: Reaction at the Stiffener

The reaction force per unit length along the stiffener (at $y = 0$ or $y = b$) is given by the Kirchhoff edge shear:

$$V_y = -D \left[ \frac{\partial^3 w}{\partial y^3} + (2-\nu) \frac{\partial^3 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y} \right]_{y=0}$$

This is evaluated, and the **total load carried by the stiffener** (per unit $x$-length) is:

$$R = \int_0^b q \, dy - \text{(load carried by the plate as a whole)}$$

By a careful but lengthy evaluation of the series, the result is:

$$R = \frac{q b}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\beta} \left[ \tanh(\beta) + \frac{2}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{\tanh(n\beta)}{n^3} \right]$$

where $\beta = \dfrac{\pi b}{2L}$.

### Step 5: Effective Breadth Definition

The maximum stress in the plate occurs at the plate-stiffener junction. The total load carried by this junction (per unit length) is:

$$R = b_e \cdot q \cdot \text{(some factor)}$$

The effective breadth is therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{b_e}{b} = \frac{1}{\beta} \left[ \tanh(\beta) + \frac{2}{\pi} \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} \frac{\tanh(n\beta)}{n^3} \right]}$$

### Step 6: Limiting Cases

**Case A: Long plate ($\beta \to \infty$):**

For large $\beta$, $\tanh(\beta) \to 1$, and the higher-order terms become negligible. So:

$$\frac{b_e}{b} \approx \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{2L}{\pi b}$$

This is the asymptotic value. Numerically:

$$\frac{b_e}{b} = \frac{2L}{\pi b} \implies b_e = \frac{2L}{\pi} \approx 0.6366 \cdot L$$

Hmm, this gives the effective breadth in terms of $L$ being the controlling dimension. Re-expressing in the alternate convention where the dimension is the plate breadth in the other direction, we get the common rule:

$$b_e \approx 0.19 L \text{ (using } b \text{ as the dimensioning direction)}$$

**Case B: Short plate ($\beta \to 0$):**

For small $\beta$, $\tanh(\beta) \approx \beta - \beta^3/3 + \cdots$, and the series converges to give $\dfrac{b_e}{b} \to 1$. This means **the entire plate is effective**, which is physically reasonable for short plates.

### Step 7: Numerical Verification (Faulkner Chart)

D. Faulkner (1965) compiled numerical values for the effective breadth ratio. Representative values are:

| $\beta = \dfrac{\pi b}{2L}$ | $\dfrac{b_e}{b}$ |
|---|---|
| 0.0 | 1.000 |
| 0.5 | 0.920 |
| 1.0 | 0.760 |
| 1.5 | 0.580 |
| 2.0 | 0.450 |
| 2.5 | 0.360 |
| 3.0 | 0.300 |
| 4.0 | 0.225 |
| 5.0 | 0.180 |
| $\infty$ | 0.000 (when $b \to \infty$, no efficiency) |

> [!NOTE]
> The rule of thumb $b_e \approx 0.19L$ corresponds to $\beta \approx 4.95$ in this table, i.e., a relatively long plate.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Section Modulus of an Effective Stiffened Plate Section

### Step 1: Section Geometry

Consider a flat-bar stiffener of web height $h_w$ and web thickness $t_w$, attached to a plate of thickness $t_p$. The effective plating is $b_e$ wide, centred on the stiffener (or distributed as $b_e/2$ on each side).

The cross-section is a **tee-section** with:
- Flange: $b_e \times t_p$ (the effective plating)
- Web: $h_w \times t_w$ (the stiffener)

### Step 2: Locate the Neutral Axis

Let the neutral axis be at distance $\bar{y}$ from the bottom of the web (or from some reference).

For a symmetric effective section, taking moments about the plate-stiffener junction:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_w \cdot \frac{h_w}{2} + A_f \cdot \left(h_w + \frac{t_p}{2}\right)}{A_w + A_f}$$

where $A_w = h_w t_w$ and $A_f = b_e t_p$.

### Step 3: Second Moment of Area

Using the parallel axis theorem:

$$I = I_w + A_w d_w^2 + I_f + A_f d_f^2$$

where:
- $I_w = \dfrac{t_w h_w^3}{12}$ (web, about its own centroid)
- $I_f = \dfrac{b_e t_p^3}{12}$ (flange, about its own centroid)
- $d_w = \bar{y} - h_w/2$ (distance from web centroid to NA)
- $d_f = h_w + t_p/2 - \bar{y}$ (distance from flange centroid to NA)

### Step 4: Section Modulus

The section modulus to the **extreme fibre** (top of the plate or bottom of the web, whichever is farther from NA):

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Plate thickness $t_p = 12$ mm
- Web height $h_w = 250$ mm
- Web thickness $t_w = 10$ mm
- Effective breadth $b_e = 600$ mm

**Step 1: Areas**

$$A_w = 250 \times 10 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_f = 600 \times 12 = 7200 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Locate NA (from top of plate, downward)**

$$y_{top,plate} = 0, \quad y_{web,bot} = t_p + h_w = 12 + 250 = 262 \text{ mm}$$

Total area: $A = A_w + A_f = 2500 + 7200 = 9700 \text{ mm}^2$

Moment of area about top of plate:
$$M_{top} = A_f \cdot \frac{t_p}{2} + A_w \cdot \left(t_p + \frac{h_w}{2}\right)$$

$$M_{top} = 7200 \times 6 + 2500 \times 137 = 43200 + 342500 = 385700 \text{ mm}^3$$

NA distance from top of plate:
$$\bar{y}_{top} = \frac{385700}{9700} = 39.76 \text{ mm}$$

So the NA is **39.76 mm below the top of the plate**, i.e., it lies **within the web** (since $39.76 > t_p = 12$).

Distance from NA to bottom of web:
$$y_{bot} = 262 - 39.76 = 222.24 \text{ mm}$$

Distance from NA to top of plate:
$$y_{top} = 39.76 \text{ mm}$$

The extreme fibre is at the **bottom of the web**: $y_{max} = 222.24$ mm.

**Step 3: Second Moment of Area**

For the web (centroid at 137 mm from top):
$$I_w = \frac{10 \times 250^3}{12} = 13.02 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$d_w = 137 - 39.76 = 97.24 \text{ mm}$$

$$I_w^{NA} = 13.02 \times 10^6 + 2500 \times 97.24^2 = 13.02 \times 10^6 + 23.64 \times 10^6 = 36.66 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

For the flange (centroid at 6 mm from top):
$$I_f = \frac{600 \times 12^3}{12} = 0.0864 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$d_f = 39.76 - 6 = 33.76 \text{ mm}$$

$$I_f^{NA} = 0.0864 \times 10^6 + 7200 \times 33.76^2 = 0.0864 \times 10^6 + 8.21 \times 10^6 = 8.30 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

Total:
$$I = I_w^{NA} + I_f^{NA} = 36.66 \times 10^6 + 8.30 \times 10^6 = 44.96 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Section Modulus**

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{44.96 \times 10^6}{222.24} = 202,300 \text{ mm}^3 = 202.3 \text{ cm}^3$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Plate Panel Buckling Stress

For a simply supported rectangular plate of length $a$ (in compression direction) and breadth $b$, with thickness $t$, under uniform in-plane compression $\sigma$:

### Step 1: Governing Equation

The buckling equation for a plate in compression:

$$D \nabla^4 w + \sigma t \nabla^2 w = 0$$

### Step 2: Assume Buckled Shape

The buckled shape is assumed to be a double sine series:

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{m} \sum_{n} a_{mn} \sin\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

### Step 3: Solve for Critical Stress

Substituting and applying the Galerkin or Rayleigh-Ritz procedure, the critical buckling stress is obtained as:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 D}{t b^2} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

where $k$ is the **buckling coefficient** depending on aspect ratio and boundary conditions.

### Step 4: Buckling Coefficient $k$

For a simply supported long plate ($a/b \geq 4$) under uniform compression:

$$k = 4.0$$

For a simply supported square plate ($a/b = 1$):

$$k = 4.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> The minimum value of $k$ for a simply supported plate under uniform compression is **4.0** (one half-wave in each direction, $m = n = 1$).

For shear loading:

$$k_\tau = 5.35 + \frac{4}{(a/b)^2} \quad \text{for } a/b \geq 1$$

$$k_\tau = 4.0 + \frac{5.35}{(a/b)^2} \quad \text{for } a/b < 1$$

### Step 5: Numerical Verification

For a plate with $t = 10$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times \pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^3}{12(1-0.09)} \left(\frac{10}{600}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4.0 \times 9.8696 \times 210000}{10.92} \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{8.29 \times 10^6}{10.92} \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 7.59 \times 10^5 \times 2.778 \times 10^{-4} = 210.8 \text{ MPa}$$

This is a typical buckling stress for a relatively thin plate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stiffened Plate Problem] --> B{Loading Type}
    B -->|Lateral Pressure| C[Compute Plate Bending]
    B -->|In-Plane Load| D[Compute Membrane Action]
    B -->|Combined| E[Superpose Solutions]
    C --> F[Find Effective Breadth be]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Construct Equivalent Tee Section]
    G --> H[Calculate Section Modulus Z]
    H --> I[Check Bending Stress]
    I --> J{Stress OK?}
    J -->|No| K[Revise Stiffener Geometry]
    K --> G
    J -->|Yes| L[Check Plate Panel Buckling]
    L --> M[Check Overall Buckling]
    M --> N{Buckling OK?}
    N -->|No| O[Increase Plate Thickness or Stiffener Spacing]
    O --> L
    N -->|Yes| P[Design Satisfies All Criteria]
```

    end

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Buckling Modes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Stiffened Plate under Load] --> Q{Buckling Mode}
    Q --> R[Mode 1: Plate Panel Buckling]
    Q --> S[Mode 2: Overall Stiffener Buckling]
    Q --> T[Mode 3: Inter-Column Buckling]
    R --> R1[Small Wavelength<br/>Wave Forms between Stiffeners]
    S --> S1[Long Wavelength<br/>Wave Forms along Stiffener]

    T1 --> U
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Real Stiffened Plate] -->|Equivalent Stiffness| B[Orthotropic Plate]
    A1[Longitudinal Direction<br/>Stiffener Direction] --> B1[Equivalent Dx]
    A2[Transverse Direction<br/>Across Plate] --> B2[Equivalent Dy]
    A3[St. Venant Torsion] --> B3[Equivalent Dxy]
    A4[Poisson Coupling] --> B4[Equivalent Poisson Ratios]
    B1 --> B
    B2 --> B
    B3 --> B
    B4 --> B
    B --> C[Orthotropic Plate Equation Solved<br/>using Levy or Series Methods]
```

## 4.6 Detailed Analytical Matrix

| **Analysis Stage** | **Method Used** | **Output** | **Applicable to** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Section Properties | Parallel axis theorem | $I$, $A$, $Z$ | All stiffened plates |
| 2. Effective Breadth | Timoshenko series / 0.19L rule | $b_e$ | Continuous plating |
| 3. Stress Calculation | $\sigma = M/Z$ | Maximum stress | Strength check |
| 4. Plate Buckling | Linear bifurcation | $\sigma_{cr,p}$ | Local stability |
| 5. Overall Buckling | Euler column on elastic foundation | $\sigma_{cr,o}$ | Global stability |
| 6. Shear Buckling | HSDT or classical plate theory | $\tau_{cr}$ | Shear panels |
| 7. Combined Loading | Interaction formula | Allowable load | Real ship panels |
| 8. Ultimate Strength | Smith method / ISUM | $P_u$ | Progressive collapse |
