# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Effective Breadth of Stiffened Plate
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Effective breadth ($b_e$) of a stiffened plate is defined as the width of plating which, when assumed to carry a stress uniformly equal to the maximum stress at the plate-stiffener junction, will transmit the same total load (or develop the same moment of resistance) as the actual non-uniform stress distribution in the plating.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\int_0^b \sigma_x(y) \, dy = b_e \cdot \sigma_{max}$$

For a long simply supported plate, $b_e \approx 0.19 L$, where $L$ is the span between transverse supports.

The effective breadth concept is fundamental in ship design because it allows the reduction of a complex 2D plate-stiffener system to a simple 1D beam problem for design and analysis purposes.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Local and Overall Buckling of a Stiffened Plate
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Local (Plate) Buckling** | **Overall Buckling** |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Plating between stiffeners | Stiffener + effective plating as column |
| **Wavelength** | Short, equal to stiffener spacing | Long, equal to span between primary supports |
| **Critical Stress** | $\sigma_{cr,p} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | $\sigma_{cr,o} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI_e}{A_e L^2}$ |
| **Controlling Parameter** | Plate slenderness $b/t$ | Column slenderness $L/r$ |
| **Failure Mode** | Plate dimples out-of-plane | Stiffener deflects laterally as a whole |
| **Prevention** | Thicker plate, closer stiffener spacing | Larger stiffener section, shorter span |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the two buckling modes. Always identify the wave pattern: short waves = local; long waves = overall. The actual critical stress is the **smaller** of the two.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Effective Breadth and Section Modulus Calculation
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A simply supported stiffened plate in a ship hull has the following particulars:
- Plate thickness, $t_p = 14$ mm
- Stiffener web height, $h_w = 300$ mm
- Stiffener web thickness, $t_w = 12$ mm
- Stiffener spacing, $s = 700$ mm
- Span between transverse supports, $L = 3.0$ m
- Hull girder bending stress at the location, $\sigma = 120$ MPa

Calculate:
**(a)** The effective breadth of the plate using the long plate approximation. (7 Marks)
**(b)** The section modulus of the effective stiffened plate section, and verify whether the given stress is within allowable limits for mild steel ($\sigma_{allow} = 175$ MPa). (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a): Effective Breadth Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the long plate criterion.

Compute the breadth parameter:

$$\beta = \frac{\pi b}{2L} = \frac{\pi \times 700}{2 \times 3000} = \frac{2199.1}{6000} = 0.3665$$

Wait — the convention must be clarified. If we treat $L = 3.0$ m as the **span between transverse supports** and $b = 700$ mm as the **stiffener spacing**, then for a *lateral pressure* scenario, the effective breadth is computed for the strip between two stiffeners.

For an **in-plane compression** scenario (hull girder bending), the formula uses the panel dimensions. The breadth parameter is:

$$\beta = \frac{\pi b}{2L} = \frac{\pi \times 700}{2 \times 3000} = 0.367$$

Since $\beta < 1$, this is **not a "long plate"** in the strict sense. We should use the full series formula or a chart.

However, applying the **commonly used approximation** $b_e \approx 0.19 L$:

$$b_e = 0.19 \times 3000 = 570 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This approximation corresponds to a long plate (large $\beta$ in the alternate convention). It is widely used in ship design offices as a first estimate. However, since the actual aspect ratio here gives $\beta < 1$, the effective breadth may be larger.

**Step 2:** More accurate calculation using Faulkner's intermediate formula.

For the in-plane loading case (hull girder bending), the relevant dimensionless parameter is:

$$\alpha = \frac{L}{b} = \frac{3000}{700} = 4.286$$

For $\alpha > 4$, the long plate approximation is appropriate:

$$b_e = \frac{2L}{\pi} (1 - \nu^2)$$

$$b_e = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi} \times (1 - 0.3^2)$$

$$b_e = \frac{6000}{3.1416} \times 0.91 = 1909.86 \times 0.91 = 1738 \text{ mm}$$

Hmm, this gives a value **larger** than the stiffener spacing, which is not physical. So we cap it at the stiffener spacing.

**Conclusion:** Take $b_e = \min(1738, 700) = 700$ mm, OR more conservatively, $b_e = 570$ mm using the design rule.

For this problem, accept the design office approximation: **$b_e = 570$ mm** (i.e., $285$ mm on each side of the stiffener).

**Answer (a):** $b_e \approx 570$ mm (or 700 mm if $L/b$ is considered long enough).

---

##### Part (b): Section Modulus and Stress Verification (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Geometry of the equivalent tee-section.

- Flange (effective plating): $b_e \times t_p = 570 \times 14$ mm
- Web: $h_w \times t_w = 300 \times 12$ mm

**Step 2:** Areas.

$$A_f = 570 \times 14 = 7980 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_w = 300 \times 12 = 3600 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_{total} = 11580 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Locate neutral axis (from top of plate).

Centroid of flange: $y_f = t_p/2 = 7$ mm
Centroid of web: $y_w = t_p + h_w/2 = 14 + 150 = 164$ mm

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_f y_f + A_w y_w}{A_{total}} = \frac{7980 \times 7 + 3600 \times 164}{11580}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{55860 + 590400}{11580} = \frac{646260}{11580} = 55.81 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Distances to extreme fibres.

- To top of plate: $y_{top} = 55.81$ mm
- To bottom of web: $y_{bot} = 314 - 55.81 = 258.19$ mm

Maximum distance: $y_{max} = 258.19$ mm (bottom of web)

**Step 5:** Second moment of area.

Flange: $I_f = \dfrac{b_e t_p^3}{12} = \dfrac{570 \times 14^3}{12} = 130,340 \text{ mm}^4$

Web: $I_w = \dfrac{t_w h_w^3}{12} = \dfrac{12 \times 300^3}{12} = 27,000,000 \text{ mm}^4$

Parallel axis:

For flange: $d_f = 55.81 - 7 = 48.81$ mm
$$I_f^{NA} = 130,340 + 7980 \times 48.81^2 = 130,340 + 19,013,500 = 19,143,840 \text{ mm}^4$$

For web: $d_w = 164 - 55.81 = 108.19$ mm
$$I_w^{NA} = 27,000,000 + 3600 \times 108.19^2 = 27,000,000 + 42,138,300 = 69,138,300 \text{ mm}^4$$

Total: $I = 19,143,840 + 69,138,300 = 88,282,140 \text{ mm}^4 = 88.28 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$

**Step 6:** Section modulus.

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{88.28 \times 10^6}{258.19} = 341,920 \text{ mm}^3 = 342 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 7:** Verify stress.

The applied stress is $\sigma = 120$ MPa. The stress developed by the bending moment corresponding to the applied stress is:

$$M = \sigma \cdot Z = 120 \times 341,920 = 41,030,400 \text{ N·mm} = 41.03 \text{ kN·m}$$

Since the applied stress 120 MPa < allowable 175 MPa, the design is **safe**.

Alternatively, if asked to verify the stress in the stiffener due to a known moment, calculate $\sigma = M/Z$ and compare.

**Answer (b):** $Z = 342$ cm³; design is adequate (120 MPa < 175 MPa allowable).

---

### Question B: Plate Buckling and Overall Stability Check
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A simply supported rectangular plate panel between stiffeners has the following particulars:
- Plate length (in direction of compression), $a = 1500$ mm
- Plate breadth, $b = 750$ mm
- Plate thickness, $t = 10$ mm
- Young's modulus, $E = 210$ GPa
- Poisson's ratio, $\nu = 0.3$
- Yield stress, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa
- Applied uniform compressive stress, $\sigma_{applied} = 80$ MPa

Calculate:
**(a)** The critical plate panel buckling stress using the classical formula, and determine the factor of safety against local buckling. (7 Marks)
**(b)** The overall Euler buckling stress of the stiffener considered as a column of length equal to the span between primary supports ($L = 3.0$ m), and compare with the local buckling stress. (7 Marks)

The stiffener has: web height $h_w = 200$ mm, web thickness $t_w = 8$ mm, effective plating $b_e = 500$ mm.

---

#### Model Solution

##### Part (a): Local Plate Panel Buckling (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Identify the loading and boundary conditions.

The plate is **simply supported** on all four edges and subjected to **uniform in-plane compression** in the $a$-direction.

**Step 2:** Calculate the aspect ratio.

$$\alpha = \frac{a}{b} = \frac{1500}{750} = 2.0$$

**Step 3:** Determine the buckling coefficient $k$.

For a simply supported plate with $a/b = 2$:

Using the formula $k = \left(\frac{a}{b} + \frac{b}{a}\right)^2$ (one half-wave each direction):

$$k = (2 + 0.5)^2 = 6.25$$

However, for $a/b = 2$, multiple buckling modes need to be checked. The minimum is found by varying $m$:

For $m = 1$ half-waves: $k_1 = (1/b \cdot 1/a + b/1 \cdot a/1)$ ... Let me use the standard formula:

$$k = \left(\frac{m a}{b} + \frac{n^2 b}{m a}\right)^2$$

For $m = 1$, $n = 1$: $k = (2 + 0.5)^2 = 6.25$
For $m = 2$, $n = 1$: $k = (4 + 0.125)^2 = 17.02$

So the minimum is $k = 6.25$ for $m = n = 1$.

**Step 4:** Calculate critical buckling stress.

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{6.25 \times \pi^2 \times 210 \times 10^3}{12 \times (1 - 0.09)} \times \left(\frac{10}{750}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{6.25 \times 9.8696 \times 210000}{10.92} \times \left(0.01333\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{6.25 \times 9.8696 \times 210000}{10.92} \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4}$$

Numerator: $6.25 \times 9.8696 \times 210000 = 12,953,850$

$$12,953,850 / 10.92 = 1,186,250$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 1,186,250 \times 1.778 \times 10^{-4} = 210.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5:** Factor of safety.

$$FoS = \frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_{applied}} = \frac{210.9}{80} = 2.64$$

This is a satisfactory factor of safety (typically $FoS \geq 1.5$ for ship plate panels).

**Answer (a):** $\sigma_{cr} = 210.9$ MPa, $FoS = 2.64$, design is safe against local buckling.

---

##### Part (b): Overall Stiffener Buckling (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Cross-section properties of the effective stiffener.

Areas:
$$A_w = h_w \times t_w = 200 \times 8 = 1600 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_f = b_e \times t = 500 \times 10 = 5000 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$A_e = A_w + A_f = 6600 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Locate neutral axis (from top of plate).

$$\bar{y} = \frac{A_f \cdot (t/2) + A_w \cdot (t + h_w/2)}{A_e}$$

$$\bar{y} = \frac{5000 \times 5 + 1600 \times 110}{6600} = \frac{25000 + 176000}{6600} = \frac{201000}{6600} = 30.45 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Second moment of area about neutral axis.

For flange:
$$I_f = \frac{500 \times 10^3}{12} = 41,667 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$d_f = 30.45 - 5 = 25.45 \text{ mm}$$
$$I_f^{NA} = 41,667 + 5000 \times 25.45^2 = 41,667 + 3,238,500 = 3,280,167 \text{ mm}^4$$

For web:
$$I_w = \frac{8 \times 200^3}{12} = 5,333,333 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$d_w = 110 - 30.45 = 79.55 \text{ mm}$$
$$I_w^{NA} = 5,333,333 + 1600 \times 79.55^2 = 5,333,333 + 10,125,160 = 15,458,493 \text{ mm}^4$$

Total: $I_e = 3,280,167 + 15,458,493 = 18,738,660 \text{ mm}^4 = 18.74 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^4$

**Step 4:** Radius of gyration.

$$r = \sqrt{\frac{I_e}{A_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{18.74 \times 10^6}{6600}} = \sqrt{2839} = 53.28 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5:** Euler buckling stress (assuming pinned-pinned, $C = 1$).

$$\sigma_{cr,o} = \frac{\pi^2 E I_e}{A_e L^2} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 210000 \times 18.74 \times 10^6}{6600 \times 3000^2}$$

$$\sigma_{cr,o} = \frac{9.8696 \times 210000 \times 18.74 \times 10^6}{6600 \times 9 \times 10^6}$$

$$\sigma_{cr,o} = \frac{3.884 \times 10^{13}}{5.94 \times 10^{10}} = 653.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with local buckling.

$$\sigma_{cr,p} = 210.9 \text{ MPa (from Part a)}$$

$$\sigma_{cr,o} = 653.9 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_{cr,p} < \sigma_{cr,o}$, the **plate panel buckling** controls. The actual critical stress is the smaller of the two:

$$\sigma_{cr,design} = \min(210.9, 653.9) = 210.9 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always compare local and overall buckling stresses. The design is governed by the **smaller** value. Many students compute only one and miss the comparison step, losing 2-3 marks. Also, in actual ship design, **inter-column buckling** (where the plate and stiffener buckle together with the elastic foundation effect) gives a critical stress **lower** than both the local and Euler values, and this is the correct ultimate buckling stress for design.

**Answer (b):** $\sigma_{cr,o} = 653.9$ MPa > $\sigma_{cr,p} = 210.9$ MPa. Local buckling controls.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up breadth parameter conventions:** Always state whether $L$ is the span between transverse supports and $b$ is the stiffener spacing, or vice versa. KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks for ambiguous notation.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to check that NA lies within the web:** If the plate is much wider than the web, the NA may be in the plate, not the web. This changes the section modulus calculation significantly.
> 
> 3. **Using full plate breadth instead of effective breadth:** This is the most common error. The full breadth gives the section modulus about 1.5 to 2 times the correct value, and therefore an unrealistically low stress. Always use $b_e$ from the formulas or the $0.19L$ rule.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting units in the final answer:** Section modulus should be in **mm³** or **cm³** (KTU prefers cm³ for ship sections). Stress should be in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
> 
> 5. **Not stating the boundary conditions:** "Simply supported" vs "clamped" gives a 4x difference in $k$. Always state assumptions.
> 
> 6. **Ignoring the symmetry of the effective section:** The effective breadth $b_e$ is split equally on both sides of the web (or sometimes taken all on one side — clarify the convention).
> 
> 7. **Missing the buckling comparison:** Always state which mode controls (local vs overall) and use the smaller value.
> 
> 8. **Forgetting the IACS rule on reduced effective plating:** Modern IACS CSR allows only a **portion** of the plating (effective flange) to be counted in the section modulus for ultimate strength checks. This is a common advanced topic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Effective breadth $b_e$** is the key concept: it converts a complex 2D plate-stiffener problem to a simple 1D beam problem.
- **Long plate approximation:** $b_e \approx 0.19 L$ (for in-plane compression with $\nu = 0.3$).
- **Short plate:** $b_e \to b$ (entire plate effective).
- **Effective breadth ratio** is always $\leq 1$.
- **Plate panel buckling** stress: $\sigma_{cr,p} = \dfrac{4\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ for simply supported, long plate under uniform compression.
- **Overall Euler buckling** stress: $\sigma_{cr,o} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I_e}{A_e L^2}$.
- **The actual critical stress** is the smaller of the two (or the inter-column buckling value for advanced design).
- **Section modulus $Z$** of a stiffened plate is calculated for the **tee-section** formed by the web + effective plating flange.
- **The neutral axis** of a typical ship stiffened plate lies in the web (not in the plate).
- **The extreme fibre** is usually at the bottom of the web (away from the plate).
- **Flexural rigidity of plate:** $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$.
- **For shipbuilding steel:** $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 235$ MPa (mild).
- **IACS CSR** uses reduced effective plating in ultimate strength assessment.
- **Modern ship design** uses orthotropic plate theory and finite element analysis for accurate results.
- **Faulkner's chart** provides numerical values of $b_e/b$ for various $\beta$.
- **Buckling coefficient $k$** depends on aspect ratio and edge conditions (minimum is 4.0 for simply supported plate in uniform compression).
- **Stiffened plate analysis** is fundamental to hull girder section modulus calculations.
- **Effective breadth** is **less** under in-plane load than under lateral load (for the same geometry).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Buckling of Stiffened Plates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNST502 - SHIP STRUCTURES I
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Buckling of Stiffened Plates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stiffened Plate (KTU Syllabus Definition):** A *stiffened plate* is a structural assembly consisting of a thin flat plating (the plate skin or base plate) reinforced on one or both sides by a series of **longitudinal stiffeners** (also called primary or secondary members) — typically rolled or built-up T-sections, L-sections, bulb flats, or flat bars — running parallel to one another and welded (or riveted, in older vessels) to the plating. Together, the plate and stiffeners act as a **composite grillage** to resist in-plane compressive, shear, and lateral loads typical of ship hull envelopes (bottom, side, deck, bulkhead plating).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Buckling of Stiffened Plates (Definition):** *Buckling* is a sudden, geometric instability failure mode where a structural member subjected to in-plane compressive (or shear) loads undergoes a large out-of-plane transverse deflection at a load level **below the material yield stress**. In a stiffened plate, buckling is *not a single event* — it manifests as one (or a combination) of three coupled instability modes: **(i) plate (panel) buckling between stiffeners**, **(ii) stiffener tripping / lateral-torsional buckling of the stiffener**, and **(iii) overall grillage (column-type) buckling of the stiffened panel as a unit**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Cardboard Bookshelf Analogy"** 📚
>
> Imagine you take a thin piece of cardboard (the **plate**) and lay it flat on a table. Push its two short edges together — it bends easily and snaps out sideways. That's **plate buckling**.
>
> Now glue a few thin plastic rulers along the length of the cardboard (the **stiffeners**). The rulers act like the spine of a book — they make the assembly vastly stiffer in the direction along them, just like the longitudinal stiffeners in a ship's hull prevent the plating from waving sideways under hull-girder bending or hydrostatic pressure.
>
> But there is a catch: the rulers themselves are slender and can **bow out sideways** between their supports (called **tripping**), or the *entire bookshelf + rulers* can buckle as a long column. So the stiffened plate has **multiple competing failure modes**, and a naval architect must check *all* of them and ensure the lowest one governs the scantling.

### 1.3 Why Buckling Matters in Ship Structures

- The plate panels in a ship hull are typically very thin compared to their span (slenderness ratio $b/t$ often between 40 and 100 for high-tensile steel).
- The material's compressive yield strength is rarely reached — the **plate wrinkles out of plane first** at a stress called the **critical buckling stress** ($\sigma_{cr}$).
- This is why **stiffeners are obligatory** in Rule-built ships: they subdivide wide plating into narrow panels (small $b$) so that $\sigma_{cr}$ becomes large, and they themselves act as load-carrying stringers in the hull-girder bending moment.
- The **design compressive stress** $\sigma_d$ used by classification societies (DNV, LR, BV, ABS, IRS) for plate and stiffener scantling checks is therefore a function of $\sigma_{cr}$ and $\sigma_y$.

### 1.4 The Three Coupled Buckling Modes — Overview

| Mode | What Buckles | Visual Cue |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Plate (Local/Inter-stiffener) Buckling** | The flat plate panel **between two adjacent stiffeners**, in the direction perpendicular to the stiffener flange tips | Plate waves sideways between stiffeners; stiffeners remain straight |
| **(b) Stiffener Tripping (Lateral–Torsional)** | The **stiffener itself** rotates/bows about its line of attachment to the plate, like a prone person rolling sideways | Stiffener flange lifts off the plate; plate may or may not participate |
| **(c) Grillage / Column Buckling (Overall)** | The **entire stiffened plate** acts as an eccentrically loaded column over several bays between transverse frames | All stiffeners in the grillage bow together in a half-wave spanning many bays |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The course emphasises **plate buckling between stiffeners** (Bryan–Stokes theory) and the **effective width concept** used to translate post-buckling reserve strength into Rule scantling formulae. Stiffener-column buckling is treated in Ship Structures II; here we focus on the plate component and the Bryan–Stokes ideal buckling stress that underwrites the **effective width $b_e$** used by classification societies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Plate Buckling Theory — Bryan (1891) and Stokes (1889)

The classic **small-deflection elastic buckling theory** for a rectangular plate under uniform in-plane compression was developed independently by G.H. Bryan (UK, 1891) and G.G. Stokes. The plate is assumed:
- Isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic (Young's modulus $E$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$).
- Thin ($t \ll b$); Kirchhoff–Love plate kinematics (neglecting transverse shear deformation).
- Subject to uniform in-plane compressive force per unit length $N_x$ in the $x$-direction.
- The deflected shape is governed by the **biharmonic plate equation** with in-plane compressive pre-stress.

For a rectangular plate simply supported on all four edges (the *Bryan boundary condition*: $w = 0$ and $M_n = 0$ at edges), the deflected buckled shape is a double sine series:

$$w(x,y) = \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_{mn} \sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right) \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

where $m$ = number of half-waves in the $x$-direction (loading direction), $n$ = number of half-waves in the $y$-direction (transverse to load), $a$ = plate length in loading direction, $b$ = plate width between stiffeners.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Build-up

1. **Equilibrium of the slightly-buckled plate** in the presence of in-plane compressive force $N_x$ yields the governing differential equation:

   $$D\!\left(\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial y^4}\right) + N_x \frac{\partial^2 w}{\partial x^2} = 0$$

   where $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate per unit width**.

2. **Substitute the assumed sinusoidal shape** $w = A_{mn}\sin(m\pi x/a)\sin(n\pi y/b)$ into the differential equation. The result is that for non-trivial $A_{mn}$, $N_x$ must equal:

   $$N_{x,cr} = \frac{\pi^2 D}{b^2}\!\left[m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^2\,\frac{a}{b}\right]^2 \quad \text{(per unit length)}$$

3. **Convert to critical stress** $\sigma_{cr} = N_{x,cr}/t$:

   $$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2\!\left[m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^2\,\frac{a}{b}\right]^2$$

4. **Minimise w.r.t. $m$ and $n$** to obtain the *lowest* critical stress (the actual buckling load). For long plates ($a/b \gg 1$), the optimum is $m=1, n=1$, giving the celebrated **long-plate buckling stress**:

   $$\sigma_{cr,\,\text{long}} = \frac{4\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi^2 E}{3(1-\nu^2)}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

5. **Short / square plates** ($a/b \le \sqrt{2}$): multiple half-waves may form in the $x$-direction; the $m \ge 2$ modes can govern. The minimum over $m$ is captured by Timoshenko's classical buckling-coefficient $k$:

   $$\sigma_{cr} = k\,\frac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

   where $k = \left[\,m\,\dfrac{b}{a} + n^2\,\dfrac{a}{b}\,\right]^{2}$ at the optimal $(m,n)$.

### 2.3 Effective Width Concept (von Kármán, 1932)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Width Principle (KEY KTU CONCEPT):** After elastic plate buckling, the plate does *not* fail. The central region of the panel sheds its load to the stiffener-supported edges, which continue to carry the post-buckling load up to yield. The **effective width** $b_e$ is the equivalent width of a uniformly stressed strip (at yield stress $\sigma_y$) that carries the same total load as the actual buckled plate (with edge strips at $\sigma_y$ and middle strip at $\sigma_{cr}$).

The classical **von Kármán effective width** formula (valid for $\sigma_y/\sigma_{cr} \ge 1$, i.e. elastically buckled plates):

$$b_e = b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}$$

This was later refined by **Winter** for cold-formed steel sections (with the 0.22 correction):

$$b_e = b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}\!\left[1 - 0.22\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in ships:** Classification society rules (DNV, LR, etc.) write the plate's contribution to the section modulus of a stiffened panel as $Z_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{b_e\,t^2}{6}$ (per stiffener spacing). The $b_e$ is a function of the *buckling coefficient* $k$, the plate slenderness $b/t$, and the material yield $\sigma_y$. This is the bridge between **buckling theory** and **scantling design**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Buckling of Stiffened Plates**

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Glossary | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $D = \dfrac{E\,t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | **Flexural rigidity** of plate per unit width; $E$ = Young's modulus, $t$ = plate thickness, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio ($\approx 0.3$ for steel) | N·m |
| F2 | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\,\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | **Bryan–Stokes critical buckling stress**; $k$ = buckling coefficient (function of $a/b$, edge conditions, load type) | Pa (N/m²) |
| F3 | $k_{\text{long}} = 4.0$ | Buckling coefficient for **long simply-supported plate** under uniaxial compression ($m=n=1$, $a/b \gg 1$) | dimensionless |
| F4 | $k_{\text{long, fixed}} = 6.97$ | Long plate with both loaded edges **clamped** (rigid stiffeners) | dimensionless |
| F5 | $k_{\text{shear}} \approx 5.35 + 4.0\,(b/a)^2$ | Buckling coefficient for **pure shear** loading (Timoshenko) | dimensionless |
| F6 | $\beta = \dfrac{b}{t}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{12(1-\nu^2)}{k\pi^2}}$ | **Plate slenderness parameter** (governs the regime: stocky $\beta < 1$, slender $\beta > 1$) | dimensionless |
| F7 | $b_e = b\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}$ | **von Kármán effective width** (elastic post-buckling) | m |
| F8 | $b_e = b\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}\!\left[1 - 0.22\,\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}\right]$ | **Winter's modified effective width** (cold-formed steel; conservative for ship use) | m |
| F9 | $Z_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{b_e\,t^{2}}{6}$ | Plate contribution to **section modulus** of stiffened panel (per stiffener spacing) | m³ |
| F10 | $\sigma_d = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{1 + \beta^4}}$ | **Design compressive stress** for plate (DNV-style lower-bound curve, also called Perry-Robertson type) | Pa |
| F11 | $\lambda = \dfrac{a}{\pi\,r}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | **Stiffener (column) slenderness**, $r = \sqrt{I/A}$, where $I$ = moment of inertia of **stiffener + effective plate flange** about the neutral axis | dimensionless |
| F12 | $\sigma_{cr,\text{col}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{A\,L_e^{2}}$ | **Euler column buckling stress** for stiffener treated as a column between transverse frames, $L_e$ = effective length | Pa |
| F13 | $K = \dfrac{N_{cr}}{\sigma_y \cdot A}$ | **Slenderness reduction factor** for stiffened plate (ratio of buckling load to squash load) | dimensionless |
| F14 | $\tau_{cr} = k_\tau\,\dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{b}\right)^2$ | **Critical shear buckling stress** of plate panel; $k_\tau$ depends on $a/b$ (F5 gives long-plate $k_\tau = 5.35$) | Pa |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Many students confuse $a$ and $b$. In Bryan–Stokes, **$a$ is the dimension in the loading direction** (length of the panel along the compressive stress), and **$b$ is the dimension perpendicular to the load** (the *spacing between stiffeners*). For ship deck/bottom plating under hull-girder bending, $b$ is therefore the **stiffener spacing** — a much smaller quantity than $a$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Classification society scantling rules** (DNV-RU-SHIP Pt.3 Ch.4, Lloyd's Register, ABS SVR, BV NR 546) are all *derived* from the Bryan–Stokes $\sigma_{cr}$ combined with the effective-width reduction.
- **Finite-element buckling analysis** in modern ship design (e.g., NASTRAN, ABAQUS, MAESTRO, FEMAP) uses the *linearised eigenvalue buckling* $\sigma_{cr}$ as the reference for imperfection-sensitivity studies and the Johnson-Ostenfeld reduction curve.
- **Ultimate strength assessment** of hull girder (progressive-collapse analysis) relies on the post-buckling effective-width reserve to model the load-shortening curve of each panel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bryan–Stokes Plate Buckling Stress

**Starting Point — Energy Formulation (Total Potential Energy Approach).**

Consider a rectangular plate of dimensions $a \times b \times t$, simply supported on all four edges, loaded by uniform in-plane compressive force per unit length $N_x$ on edges $x=0$ and $x=a$.

**Step 1 — Strain Energy from Bending.**
For Kirchhoff plate theory, the strain energy of bending per unit area is:

$$U = \frac{D}{2}\!\left[\left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)^{2} - 2(1-\nu)\!\left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}}\,\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}} - \left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x\partial y}\right)^{2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Work Done by the In-Plane Compressive Force.**
The applied force $N_x$ does work as the plate deflects laterally (because the ends move closer by the shortening of the buckled shape):

$$V = -\frac{N_x}{2}\int_{0}^{a}\!\int_{0}^{b}\!\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^{2}dx\,dy$$

**Step 3 — Assume Sinusoidal Buckled Shape.**
For simply supported edges satisfying $w = 0$, $M_x = 0$ at $x = 0, a$ and $w = 0$, $M_y = 0$ at $y = 0, b$:

$$w(x,y) = A_{mn}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Integrals.**

Strain energy integral evaluates to:

$$\int_{0}^{a}\!\int_{0}^{b}\!\left(\frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}w}{\partial y^{2}}\right)^{2}dx\,dy = \frac{A_{mn}^{2}\,\pi^{4}ab}{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}$$

And the second mixed term $\int(\partial^{2}w/\partial x^{2})(\partial^{2}w/\partial y^{2})$ reduces to $\dfrac{A_{mn}^{2}\,m^{2}n^{2}\pi^{4}}{4ab}$.

Likewise, the work integral:

$$\int_{0}^{a}\!\int_{0}^{b}\!\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)^{2}dx\,dy = \frac{A_{mn}^{2}\,m^{2}\pi^{2}\,b}{4a}$$

**Step 5 — Total Potential Energy.**

$$\Pi = U + V = \frac{D\,\pi^{4}ab}{8}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}A_{mn}^{2} - \frac{N_x\,m^{2}\pi^{2}\,b}{8a}A_{mn}^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Stationarity Condition $\partial \Pi / \partial A_{mn} = 0$.**

$$\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial A_{mn}} = \frac{D\,\pi^{4}ab}{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}A_{mn} - \frac{N_x\,m^{2}\pi^{2}\,b}{4a}A_{mn} = 0$$

For non-trivial $A_{mn} \neq 0$:

$$\frac{D\,\pi^{4}ab}{4}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2} = \frac{N_x\,m^{2}\pi^{2}\,b}{4a}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $N_{x,cr}$.**

$$N_{x,cr} = \frac{D\,\pi^{2}\,a^{2}}{b^{2}}\!\left(\frac{m^{2}}{a^{2}} + \frac{n^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)^{2}\frac{b^{2}}{m^{2}}$$

After rearranging:

$$N_{x,cr} = \frac{\pi^{2}D}{b^{2}}\!\left(m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to Critical Stress.**

Divide by plate thickness $t$ and substitute $D = \dfrac{Et^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{x,cr}}{t} = \frac{\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\!\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}\!\left(m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2}$$

Define the **buckling coefficient** $k(m,n,a/b)$ such that:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = k\,\frac{\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\!\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}, \quad k = \left(m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

**Step 9 — Minimum over $m$ and $n$.**
For a long plate ($a/b \to \infty$), the bracketed expression is minimised at $m=1, n=1$, giving $k_{\min} = 4$.

For finite $a/b$, the optimum $m$ is $m = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)$. The minimum $k$ as a function of $\alpha = a/b$ for simply supported edges under uniaxial compression (Bryan):

$$k_{\min}(\alpha) = \begin{cases} 4 & \alpha \le \sqrt{2} \\[4pt] \left(\dfrac{1}{\alpha} + \alpha\right)^{2} & \alpha > \sqrt{2} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric interpretation:** $a/b \le \sqrt{2}$: buckling is one half-wave along length (the "column mode"). $a/b > \sqrt{2}$: multiple half-waves develop; the plate subdivides itself into nearly square cells — this is what you see when you look at the bottom of a derelict ship that has lost its stiffeners.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Critical Buckling Stress of a Stiffened Plate Panel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (KTU Board-style 7-mark sub-question):**

**Problem:** A simply-supported plate panel between two stiffeners has dimensions $a = 1500$ mm (length along load), $b = 600$ mm (stiffener spacing), $t = 12$ mm. Material is mild steel with $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mm². Calculate:
(a) The elastic critical buckling stress $\sigma_{cr}$,
(b) The von Kármán effective width $b_e$,
(c) Whether the plate is in the slender or stocky regime (compute $\beta$).

**Solution:**

Given: $a = 1500$ mm, $b = 600$ mm, $t = 12$ mm, $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$.

**Step (a): Buckling coefficient and $\sigma_{cr}$.**

Compute the aspect ratio: $\alpha = a/b = 1500/600 = 2.5$.

Since $\alpha = 2.5 > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, multiple half-waves may form. Try $m = 3$ (closest integer to $\alpha$):

$$k = \left(m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2} = \left(3 \cdot \frac{600}{1500} + 1^{2} \cdot \frac{1500}{600}\right)^{2}$$

$$k = \left(1.2 + 2.5\right)^{2} = (3.7)^{2} = 13.69$$

Check $m = 2$: $k = \left(2 \cdot 0.4 + 2.5\right)^{2} = (0.8 + 2.5)^{2} = (3.3)^{2} = 10.89$.

Check $m = 4$: $k = \left(4 \cdot 0.4 + 2.5\right)^{2} = (1.6 + 2.5)^{2} = (4.1)^{2} = 16.81$.

**Minimum $k$ occurs at $m = 2$, $n = 1$:** $k_{\min} = 10.89$.

Now compute $\sigma_{cr}$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = k_{\min}\,\frac{\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\,\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}$$

Numerator constant: $\dfrac{\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})} = \dfrac{(9.8696)(2.06 \times 10^{5})}{12(1 - 0.09)} = \dfrac{2.0331 \times 10^{6}}{10.92} = 1.862 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².

Slenderness squared: $(t/b)^{2} = (12/600)^{2} = (0.02)^{2} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$\sigma_{cr} = 10.89 \times 1.862 \times 10^{5} \times 4.0 \times 10^{-4} = 10.89 \times 74.48 = 810.9 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

But $\sigma_{cr}$ cannot exceed yield: $\sigma_{cr, \text{actual}} = \min(\sigma_{cr}, \sigma_y) = \min(810.9, 235) = 235$ N/mm².

So the plate does *not* buckle elastically — it yields first. This is the **stocky regime**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is unusual for ship plate (typically $b/t = 50$ gives $\sigma_{cr} \approx 70$ N/mm²). For a typical bottom plate with $b = 750$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $a = 2000$ mm, $k_{\min} \approx 4$, $\sigma_{cr} \approx 70$–80 N/mm² — definitely slender. Our example deliberately uses a thick plate to illustrate the regime change.

**Step (b): Effective width.**
Since $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$, the plate does not buckle elastically, so $b_e = b = 600$ mm (full effectiveness).

**Step (c): Slenderness parameter $\beta$.**

$$\beta = \frac{b}{t}\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_y}{E}}\,\sqrt{\frac{12(1-\nu^{2})}{k\pi^{2}}}$$

$$\beta = \frac{600}{12}\,\sqrt{\frac{235}{2.06 \times 10^{5}}}\,\sqrt{\frac{10.92}{10.89 \times 9.8696}}$$

$$\beta = 50 \times \sqrt{1.141 \times 10^{-3}}\,\sqrt{\frac{10.92}{107.5}}$$

$$\beta = 50 \times 0.03378 \times \sqrt{0.1016} = 50 \times 0.03378 \times 0.3187$$

$$\beta = 50 \times 0.01077 = 0.538$$

Since $\beta < 1$, plate is **stocky** — confirming step (a) result.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** When $\beta < 1$, the plate reaches yield before buckling; full section is effective. When $\beta > 1$, the plate buckles elastically and only the effective width $b_e < b$ carries design load. KTU often asks students to identify the regime and apply the correct formula — common exam pitfall is applying $b_e$ blindly even when $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Buckling Modes

| Property | Plate Buckling | Stiffener Tripping | Grillage Buckling |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Component that buckles** | Plate skin between stiffeners | Stiffener flange/web laterally | Whole stiffened plate over multiple bays |
| **Half-wave length** | $\approx b$ (panel width) | $\approx b$ (between adjacent plate nodes) | $\approx L$ between transverse frames |
| **Governing equation** | Bryan–Stokes $\sigma_{cr}$ | Lateral-torsional beam theory | Euler column + plate participation |
| **Stress to compare** | $\sigma_{cr,\text{plate}}$ vs $\sigma_d$ | $\sigma_{cr,\text{trip}}$ vs $\sigma_d$ | $\sigma_{cr,\text{col}}$ vs $\sigma_d$ |
| **Remedy** | Reduce $b$ (closer stiffener spacing) or increase $t$ | Larger flange width; weld to plate; reduce $b$ | Stiffener with larger $I$ (deeper section); reduce frame spacing |
| **Typical design code** | DNV Pt.3 Sec.4 (plate) | DNV Pt.3 Sec.5 (stiffeners) | DNV Pt.3 Sec.5 (ultimate strength) |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of Stiffened-Plate Buckling Analysis

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the logical workflow used in ship structural design to assess buckling of a stiffened plate panel — the same flow a classification society rule applies (DNV, LR, ABS).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Plate Geometry a b t] --> B[Compute Plate Flexural Rigidity D]
    B --> C[Determine Edge Conditions]
    C --> D{Simply Supported or Clamped?}
    D -- SS Simply Supported --> E[Apply Bryan Stokes Formula]
    D -- CL Clamped --> F[Apply Timoshenko k Table]
    E --> G[Compute sigma_cr plate]
    F --> G[Compute sigma_cr plate]
    G --> H[Compare sigma_cr with sigma_y]
    H -- sigma_cr greater sigma_y --> I[Stocky Regime]
    H -- sigma_cr less sigma_y --> J[Slender Regime]
    I --> K[Use Full Width b in Section Modulus]
    J --> L[Compute Effective Width b_e von Karman]
    L --> M[Compute Section Modulus Z plate]
    K --> N[Compute Total Z including Stiffener]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Bending Stress sigma]
    O --> P{sigma less sigma_d Allowable}
    P -- Yes --> Q[PASS Adequate Buckling Resistance]
    P -- No --> R[FAIL Revise Scantlings]
    R --> S[Increase t or Reduce b or a]
    S --> A
```

**Reading the flow:**
1. Inputs are plate geometry and material.
2. Edge condition selection determines whether to use $k = 4$ (simply supported long plate) or $k = 6.97$ (clamped).
3. $\sigma_{cr}$ is computed and compared to $\sigma_y$.
4. The plate's regime decides whether to use the *full width* or the *effective width* in section modulus.
5. Total section modulus (plate + stiffener) is used in hull-girder bending check.
6. If computed bending stress exceeds allowable design stress $\sigma_d$, the design is iterated.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Three Coupled Buckling Modes of a Stiffened Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph StiffenedPlateAssembly[Stiffened Plate Assembly]
        direction TB
        PlateSkin[Flat Plate Skin thickness t]
        Stiffener1[Longitudinal Stiffener 1 T Section]
        Stiffener2[Longitudinal Stiffener 2 T Section]
        PlateSkin --- Stiffener1
        PlateSkin --- Stiffener2
    end
    StiffenedPlateAssembly --> Mode1Buckling
    Mode1Buckling[Mode 1 Local Plate Buckling between Stiffeners]
    StiffenedPlateAssembly --> Mode2Buckling
    Mode2Buckling[Mode 2 Stiffener Tripping lateral torsional]
    StiffenedPlateAssembly --> Mode3Buckling
    Mode3Buckling[Mode 3 Grillage Column Buckling over many bays]
    Mode1Buckling --> DesignCheck
    Mode2Buckling --> DesignCheck
    Mode3Buckling --> DesignCheck
    DesignCheck[Design Check Minimum sigma_cr governs scantling]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Buckled Shape Wave Patterns

The number of half-waves that form in the plate during buckling (parameter $m$) directly depends on the aspect ratio $a/b$. The following schematic shows the buckled shape in plan view for three aspect ratios:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LowAlpha[Low aspect ratio a over b less than 1.414]
        A1[Single half wave in x direction] --> A2[Plate bows out as one big bulge]
    end
    subgraph MediumAlpha[Medium aspect ratio a over b between 1.414 and 3]
        B1[Two or three half waves form in x direction] --> B2[Plate subdivides into cells]
    end
    subgraph HighAlpha[High aspect ratio a over b greater than 3]
        C1[Many half waves n equals a over b approximately] --> C2[Plate looks like corrugated sheet]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** Long slender panels (typical of ship side plating where $a/b$ can exceed 5) develop a "corrugated" buckled pattern with many transverse waves — this is the *optimum* mode Nature selects to minimise critical stress. Ship-side plating often shows exactly this pattern in damaged condition.

### 4.4 Effective Width Physical Picture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BeforeBuckling[Before Buckling uniform stress sigma]
        U1[Full width b uniform stress sigma less than sigma_y]
    end
    subgraph AfterBuckling[After Elastic Buckling post critical]
        V1[Edge strips width b_e each side carry stress sigma_y]
        V2[Middle strip width b minus 2 b_e carries lower stress]
    end
    BeforeBuckling --> AfterBuckling
    V1 --> W1[Equivalent effective width b_e at yield sigma_y]
    V2 --> W1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** The *effective width* is a fictitious concept: it represents an equivalent *uniformly-yielded* strip of width $b_e < b$ that carries the same total in-plane force as the actual buckled plate (with yielded edges and buckled middle). This lets us keep using linear elastic section-modulus formulae even after the plate has buckled.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Pattern Note:** Part A carries 3 marks each, expects crisp definition / short derivation / formula application. Answers should fit in roughly one page of the answer booklet.

#### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *stiffened plate*. With the help of a sketch, explain the three possible modes of buckling in a stiffened plate assembly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stiffened plate** is a structural assembly in which a thin flat plate is reinforced along one direction by a series of parallel stiffeners (T-sections, L-sections, bulb flats, etc.) welded to it, so that together they resist in-plane compressive, shear, and lateral loads efficiently. It is the primary load-carrying envelope element in ship hulls, decks, and bulkheads.

**The three buckling modes are:**

1. **Local plate (panel) buckling** — the flat plate wrinkles sideways *between two adjacent stiffeners*. Governed by Bryan–Stokes theory.
2. **Stiffener tripping** — the stiffener itself rotates laterally about its line of attachment, like a prone person rolling sideways. A lateral-torsional instability.
3. **Grillage (overall column) buckling** — the entire stiffened plate acts as a column over many bays between transverse frames, and bows out in a long half-wave.

The *lowest* of these critical stresses governs the design.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students write only *two* modes (plate and overall). KTU explicitly tests knowledge of **all three**, including stiffener tripping. Loss of 1 mark for missing one mode is very common.

---

#### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** State the **von Kármán effective width formula** for a plate that has buckled elastically, and explain its physical significance in ship structural design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Formula:**

$$b_e = b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}$$

where $b$ = actual plate width (stiffener spacing), $\sigma_{cr}$ = elastic buckling stress (Bryan–Stokes), $\sigma_y$ = material yield stress.

**Physical significance:** Once a plate panel buckles, the central portion of the plate loses stiffness and sheds load to the stiffener-supported edges, which continue to carry stress up to yield. The *effective width* $b_e$ is an *equivalent* uniformly-yielded strip that would carry the same total in-plane load as the actual buckled panel. It allows designers to compute the plate's contribution to the hull section modulus using:

$$Z_{\text{plate}} = \dfrac{b_e\,t^{2}}{6}$$

In ship structural design, $b_e$ forms the bridge between *linear elastic buckling theory* and *practical scantling rules* used by classification societies (DNV, LR, ABS, BV). It accounts for the *post-buckling reserve strength* of the plate.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse $\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y$ with $\sigma_y/\sigma_{cr}$ (inverted ratio). The correct ratio is **$\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y$** under the square root — remember "**cr**itical under the root". Inverting it gives wrong (nonsense > 1) answers.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. For Module 4 topics, expect either (a) full derivation + (b) numerical application, OR (a) concept + (b) design check.

---

#### 📘 Question B.1 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Set 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **Bryan–Stokes critical buckling stress** for a simply-supported rectangular plate under uniform in-plane compressive loading. Clearly state the assumptions, governing differential equation, deflected shape, and the final expression for $\sigma_{cr}$ in terms of the buckling coefficient $k$.

**(b) [7 marks]** A ship bottom plate between two adjacent longitudinal stiffeners is simply supported on all four edges. The panel dimensions are: length $a = 2400$ mm (along the hull girder direction), width $b = 800$ mm (stiffener spacing), thickness $t = 14$ mm. Material properties: $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mm². Determine:
   (i) the buckling coefficient $k$,
   (ii) the elastic critical stress $\sigma_{cr}$,
   (iii) the von Kármán effective width $b_e$,
   (iv) whether the plate is in the slender or stocky regime.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions (1 mark):* Kirchhoff thin-plate theory (transverse shear deformation neglected), isotropic linear elastic material, small deflection theory, simply-supported edges with $w = 0$ and $M_n = 0$, uniform in-plane compressive loading $N_x$.

*Governing equation (2 marks):*

$$D\!\left(\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{4}} + 2\,\frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial x^{2}\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{4} w}{\partial y^{4}}\right) + N_x\,\frac{\partial^{2} w}{\partial x^{2}} = 0$$

*Deflected shape and substitution (2 marks):*

$$w(x,y) = A_{mn}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{m\pi x}{a}\right)\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi y}{b}\right)$$

Substituting and applying the non-triviality condition yields:

$$N_{x,cr} = \frac{\pi^{2}D}{b^{2}}\!\left(m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2}$$

*Final expression (2 marks):* Dividing by $t$ and using $D = Et^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k\,\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}\!\left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^{2}, \quad k = \left(m\,\frac{b}{a} + n^{2}\,\frac{a}{b}\right)^{2}}$$

For long plates, $k_{\min} = 4$ at $m = n = 1$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Aspect ratio:* $\alpha = a/b = 2400/800 = 3.0$. Since $\alpha > \sqrt{2}$, try $m = 3$:

$$k = \left(3 \cdot \frac{800}{2400} + 1^{2} \cdot \frac{2400}{800}\right)^{2} = (1.0 + 3.0)^{2} = 16.0$$

Check $m = 4$: $k = (4 \cdot 0.333 + 3.0)^{2} = (1.333 + 3.0)^{2} = (4.333)^{2} = 18.78$.

Check $m = 2$: $k = (2 \cdot 0.333 + 3.0)^{2} = (0.667 + 3.0)^{2} = (3.667)^{2} = 13.44$.

**Minimum $k = 13.44$ at $m = 2$, $n = 1$.** **(1.5 marks)**

*Step 2 — Critical stress:* Using $\pi^{2}/[12(1-\nu^{2})] = 9.8696/10.92 = 0.9037$:

$$\sigma_{cr} = 13.44 \times 0.9037 \times 2.06 \times 10^{5} \times (14/800)^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 13.44 \times 0.9037 \times 2.06 \times 10^{5} \times 3.0625 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 13.44 \times 57.04 = 766.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

But **capped at yield**: $\sigma_{cr, \text{actual}} = \min(766.6, 235) = 235$ N/mm². **(2 marks)**

*Step 3 — Effective width:* Since the computed $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$, the plate does not buckle elastically — it yields first. Therefore the **full width is effective**:

$$b_e = b = 800 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{plate is stocky}) \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

*Step 4 — Regime check:* Slenderness parameter:

$$\beta = \frac{b}{t}\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_y}{E}}\sqrt{\frac{12(1-\nu^{2})}{k\pi^{2}}} = \frac{800}{14}\sqrt{\frac{235}{2.06 \times 10^{5}}}\sqrt{\frac{10.92}{13.44 \times 9.8696}}$$

$$\beta = 57.14 \times 0.03378 \times \sqrt{0.0823} = 57.14 \times 0.03378 \times 0.2869 = 0.554$$

Since $\beta = 0.554 < 1$, **plate is stocky**. **(2 marks)**

**Conclusion:** The 14-mm bottom plate with 800-mm stiffener spacing is in the stocky regime. Yielding governs; no elastic buckling occurs and $b_e = b$. Designers may consider *thinning* the plate to save weight (e.g., $t = 10$ mm would give $\beta > 1$ and put it into the slender buckling regime — but then $b_e$ reduction must be applied).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes on this type of question:
> 1. Forgetting to **cap $\sigma_{cr}$ at $\sigma_y$** (2 marks lost).
> 2. Using $\alpha = b/a$ instead of $a/b$ in the formula (yields wrong $k$).
> 3. Computing $b_e$ with the **inverted** ratio $\sqrt{\sigma_y/\sigma_{cr}}$ instead of $\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y}$.
> 4. Skipping the regime check — KTU frequently awards 2 marks just for correctly identifying slender vs stocky.

---

#### 📘 Question B.2 (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **effective width concept** of von Kármán for a plate that has buckled elastically. Sketch the stress distribution across the width of a buckled plate and show how the effective width $b_e$ is derived. Write down Winter's modification and explain why it is sometimes preferred in design.

**(b) [7 marks]** A deck plate panel between two T-stiffeners has the following data: $a = 1800$ mm, $b = 700$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $E = 2.10 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 315$ N/mm² (high-tensile steel). Calculate:
   (i) the elastic critical stress $\sigma_{cr}$,
   (ii) the effective width $b_e$ using (1) von Kármán and (2) Winter's formula,
   (iii) the plate contribution to section modulus $Z_{\text{plate}}$ in each case.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Effective Width Concept (7 marks):**

After elastic buckling of a simply-supported plate under in-plane compression, the plate does *not* collapse. The stiffener-supported edge strips continue to carry the load up to yield stress $\sigma_y$, while the central strip carries a much lower average stress because it has buckled into waves. The actual stress distribution across the width is non-uniform: high at edges, low in the middle (Sketch required — 2 marks).

**Derivation of von Kármán $b_e$ (3 marks):**

Equating the total load carried by the buckled plate to the load in an equivalent uniformly-yielded strip of width $b_e$:

$$\sigma_y \cdot b_e \cdot t = \sigma_y \cdot 2 \cdot (b_e/2) \cdot t + \sigma_{cr} \cdot (b - b_e) \cdot t$$

But more rigorously, using the post-buckling stress distribution derived from large-deflection theory:

$$\sigma_{y, \text{edge}} \cdot b_e \cdot t = \sigma_y \cdot b_e \cdot t = N_{total}$$

For elastic post-buckling, the average stress in the middle strip equals $\sigma_{cr}$ (a classical result). Equating total loads:

$$\sigma_y \cdot b_e = \sigma_{cr} \cdot b \quad\Rightarrow\quad \boxed{b_e = b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}}$$

**Winter's formula (1 mark):**

$$b_e = b\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}\!\left[1 - 0.22\,\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{cr}}{\sigma_y}}\right]$$

This was developed empirically by Winter (1947) for cold-formed steel members to account for **initial geometric imperfections** (initial out-of-flatness), which reduce the effective width relative to the ideal von Kármán value. It is more conservative and is used in ship-class rules where imperfections cannot be guaranteed to be negligible.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1:* $\alpha = a/b = 1800/700 = 2.571$. Since $\alpha > \sqrt{2}$, try $m = 3$: $k = (3 \cdot 700/1800 + 1 \cdot 1800/700)^{2} = (1.167 + 2.571)^{2} = (3.738)^{2} = 13.97$.

Try $m = 2$: $k = (2 \cdot 0.389 + 2.571)^{2} = (0.778 + 2.571)^{2} = (3.349)^{2} = 11.22$.

**$k_{\min} = 11.22$ at $m = 2, n = 1$.** **(2 marks)**

*Step 2:* $\sigma_{cr} = 11.22 \times \pi^2 \times 2.10 \times 10^5 / [12(1-0.09)] \times (10/700)^2$

$$= 11.22 \times 0.9037 \times 2.10 \times 10^{5} \times 2.041 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 11.22 \times 38.74 = 434.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

Since $\sigma_{cr} = 434.6 > \sigma_y = 315$ N/mm², plate is **stocky** → full width effective. **(1.5 marks)**

*Step 3:* Compute $\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y = 434.6/315 = 1.38$. But this is **greater than 1** — meaning $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$. This is *unphysical* in the effective-width formulation (effective width is meaningful only when plate buckles elastically, i.e., $\sigma_{cr} < \sigma_y$). 

Therefore, the formal result is **$b_e = b$ (full width effective)** under both von Kármán and Winter. The plate is in the **stocky regime**. **(1 mark)**

*Step 4 — Section modulus:*

$$Z_{\text{plate, von Karman}} = Z_{\text{plate, Winter}} = \frac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6} = \frac{700 \times 10^{2}}{6} = 11667 \text{ mm}^{3} \approx 11.67 \text{ cm}^{3} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Note to student:** This question is intentionally constructed to highlight that **when $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y$, the effective-width concept does not apply**, and the plate is governed by yielding, not buckling. In a real exam, students should explicitly state this regime change.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** If a student writes "$b_e = b\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y}$ with $\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y > 1$, so $b_e > b$" — this is **wrong**. The von Kármán formula is *derived* under the assumption $\sigma_{cr} < \sigma_y$. Outside this range, it must be replaced by $b_e = b$. 2 marks lost for missing this regime boundary.

---

### 5.3 Alternative Choice — Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **three possible buckling modes** of a stiffened plate. Discuss the role of the *buckling coefficient $k$* in determining the elastic critical stress.

**(b) [7 marks]** A side-shell plate between two longitudinal stiffeners has: $a = 3000$ mm, $b = 900$ mm, $t = 11$ mm. Material: $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $\nu = 0.3$, $\sigma_y = 235$ N/mm². Calculate $\sigma_{cr}$, the effective width $b_e$, and identify the buckling regime. Comment on whether this scantling is efficient (i.e., would reducing $t$ to 9 mm still be safe?).

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- Sketch showing stiffened plate + 3 buckled shapes (1.5 marks).
- Description of (i) plate buckling, (ii) stiffener tripping, (iii) grillage buckling (3 marks).
- Discussion of buckling coefficient $k$: $k$ is a dimensionless factor that accounts for aspect ratio, edge conditions (SS vs CL), and load type (uniaxial vs shear vs biaxial); $k$ multiplies the long-plate simply-supported baseline value of 4 (2.5 marks).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Aspect ratio:* $\alpha = 3000/900 = 3.33$. Try $m = 3, n = 1$: $k = (3 \cdot 0.3 + 3.33)^{2} = (0.9 + 3.33)^{2} = (4.23)^{2} = 17.89$.

Try $m = 4, n = 1$: $k = (4 \cdot 0.3 + 3.33)^{2} = (1.2 + 3.33)^{2} = 20.5$.

Try $m = 2, n = 1$: $k = (2 \cdot 0.3 + 3.33)^{2} = (0.6 + 3.33)^{2} = 15.44$.

**Minimum $k = 15.44$ at $m = 2$.**

Critical stress: $\sigma_{cr} = 15.44 \times 0.9037 \times 2.06 \times 10^{5} \times (11/900)^{2} = 15.44 \times 0.9037 \times 2.06 \times 10^{5} \times 1.494 \times 10^{-4} = 15.44 \times 27.83 = 429.7$ N/mm².

Since $\sigma_{cr} > \sigma_y = 235$ N/mm², **plate is stocky → $b_e = b = 900$ mm**.

**Regime check:** $\beta = (900/11) \times \sqrt{235/(2.06 \times 10^{5})} \times \sqrt{10.92/(15.44 \times 9.87)} = 81.82 \times 0.03378 \times 0.268 = 0.741$.

Since $\beta = 0.741 < 1$, **stocky confirmed**.

**Comment on efficiency:** The plate is in the stocky regime — material is over-utilised for buckling resistance. Reducing $t$ to 9 mm:

$\sigma_{cr}$ for $t = 9$ mm scales as $t^2$: $\sigma_{cr}(t=9) = 429.7 \times (9/11)^2 = 429.7 \times 0.669 = 287.5$ N/mm².

Still greater than $\sigma_y = 235$ → still stocky → still safe.

Reduce further to $t = 8$ mm: $\sigma_{cr} = 429.7 \times (8/11)^2 = 429.7 \times 0.529 = 227.5$ N/mm² $< \sigma_y$ → now slender regime.

$\beta = (900/8) \times 0.03378 \times 0.268 = 112.5 \times 0.03378 \times 0.268 = 1.018$.

Just over 1 — **borderline slender**. Compute $b_e$:

$b_e = b\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_y} = 900 \times \sqrt{227.5/235} = 900 \times 0.984 = 885.6$ mm.

Plate section modulus: $Z_{\text{plate}} = 885.6 \times 8^2 / 6 = 9446$ mm³ (vs $b t^2/6 = 900 \times 64/6 = 9600$ mm³ for full width). About 1.6% reduction — practically negligible.

**Conclusion:** Plate scantling is conservative; the shipyard may safely reduce from 11 mm to 8 mm without significant loss of effectiveness, saving ~27% plating weight.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Mode 3 — Grillage Buckling** is often the *least* explained in answers. KTU examiners penalise 2 marks if it's described vaguely. Use precise language: *"over multiple bays between transverse frames, like an eccentrically loaded column."*
> 2. **Sign convention** for the buckling coefficient $k$: positive compressive $N_x$ produces buckling; $k$ is always **positive** and **dimensionless**.
> 3. **Units consistency:** Use mm and N/mm² (MPa) for steel plate problems — KTU valuation scripts expect SI consistency.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for "Buckling of Stiffened Plates" (KTU Module 4)**

**🔹 Core Concepts**
- A stiffened plate has **three coupled buckling modes**: (i) plate panel, (ii) stiffener tripping, (iii) grillage column.
- Plate buckling is governed by **Bryan–Stokes theory** (1891).
- Stiffener tripping is a **lateral-torsional** mode; column buckling is **Euler-type** over many bays.
- The **lowest** of the three critical stresses governs the design.

**🔹 Key Formulae**
- Bryan–Stokes critical stress: $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{k\,\pi^{2}E}{12(1-\nu^{2})}(t/b)^{2}$
- Buckling coefficient $k$ depends on aspect ratio $a/b$ and edge conditions; $k = 4$ for simply supported long plate.
- Flexural rigidity: $D = Et^{3}/[12(1-\nu^{2})]$.
- Von Kármán effective width: $b_{e} = b\,\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_{y}}$.
- Winter's modification: $b_{e} = b\,\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_{y}}\,(1 - 0.22\sqrt{\sigma_{cr}/\sigma_{y}})$.
- Plate section modulus: $Z_{\text{plate}} = b_{e}t^{2}/6$.
- Slenderness parameter: $\beta = (b/t)\sqrt{\sigma_{y}/E}\sqrt{12(1-\nu^{2})/(k\pi^{2})}$.

**🔹 Critical Distinctions (often tested)**
- **$a$** = plate length along load direction; **$b$** = width perpendicular to load (stiffener spacing).
- **Stocky** plate ($\beta < 1$): yields before buckling; $b_e = b$; full section effective.
- **Slender** plate ($\beta > 1$): buckles elastically; $b_e < b$; effective width required.
- Effective width formula is **only valid for $\sigma_{cr} < \sigma_y$**.
- $\sigma_{cr}$ in formula is the **linearised elastic** critical stress; actual plate behaviour is post-buckled.

**🔹 Practical Values to Remember**
- $\nu$ for steel ≈ **0.3** → $1-\nu^2 \approx 0.91$.
- $12(1-\nu^2) \approx 10.92$ for steel.
- $\pi^{2} \approx 9.8696$.
- $\pi^{2}E/[12(1-\nu^{2})]$ for $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² ≈ **1.86 × 10⁵ N/mm²**.
- Long-plate baseline: $k = 4$ (SS), $k = 6.97$ (CL), $k = 5.35$ (shear).

**🔹 Common Mistakes to Avoid**
- Forgetting to cap $\sigma_{cr}$ at $\sigma_y$.
- Using $b/a$ instead of $a/b$ in $k$.
- Inverting the ratio under the square root in $b_e$.
- Forgetting one of the three buckling modes (especially **tripping**).
- Not drawing sketches — KTU valuation awards 1–2 marks for diagrams.

**🔹 Bridge to Higher Courses**
- The **effective width** concept is the seed of **ultimate strength analysis** in Ship Structures II and III (Faulkner's formula, Paik's ISUM plates, Smith's method).
- Stiffener tripping transitions into **lateral-torsional buckling** of beams in Advanced Structural Mechanics.
- Grillage buckling is the foundational idea for **plate-girder bridge deck analysis** in Civil Engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Topic: Buckling of Stiffened Plates.** For Ship Structures II (PCNST503), the analysis extends to **ultimate (post-buckling) strength** and **progressive collapse** of the hull girder — these are direct descendants of the elastic buckling and effective-width concepts covered here.
